1	classdef nrComponentCarrierContext < handle
2	    %nrComponentCarrierContext Carrier-specific information of a UE at gNB scheduler
3	    %
4	    %   This class reprsents the information which scheduler maintains for a
5	    %   connected UE on a particular carrier.
6	    %
7	    %   Note: This is an internal undocumented class and its API and/or
8	    %   functionality may change in subsequent releases.
9	
10	    %   Copyright 2024 The MathWorks, Inc.
11	
12	    properties(SetAccess=private)
13	        %NumTransmitAntennas Number of transmit antennas at the UE
14	        NumTransmitAntennas
15	
16	        %NumReceiveAntennas Number of receive antennas at the UE
17	        NumReceiveAntennas
18	
19	        %CSIRSConfiguration CSI-RS configuration of the UE
20	        CSIRSConfiguration
21	
22	        %SRSConfiguration SRS configuration of the UE
23	        SRSConfiguration
24	
25	        %CSIMeasurementDL Wideband DL channel measurement for the UE node based on CSI-RS and SRS
26	        % It is specified as a structure with these fields.
27	        %
28	        % CSIRS вЂ” A structure with these fields:
29	        %      RI вЂ” Rank indicator
30	        %      PMISet вЂ” Precoder matrix indicator (PMI) set
31	        %      CQI вЂ” Channel quality indicator
32	        %      W вЂ” Precoding matrix
33	        %
34	        % SRS вЂ” A structure with these fields:
35	        %       RI вЂ” Rank indicator
36	        %       W вЂ” Precoding matrix
37	        %       MCSIndex вЂ” Modulation and coding scheme index
38	        CSIMeasurementDL
39	
40	        %CSIMeasurementUL UL CSI measurements based on SRS configured
41	        % It is a structure with the fields: 'RI', 'TPMI', 'MCSIndex'
42	        CSIMeasurementUL
43	    end
44	
45	    properties(Hidden)
46	        %RetransmissionContextDL Information about downlink retransmission requirements
47	        % 1-by-P matrix where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. It stores the
48	        % information of HARQ processes for which the reception failed at gNB. This
49	        % information is used for assigning downlink assignments for
50	        % retransmissions. 1 indicates that the reception has failed for this
51	        % particular HARQ process ID governed by the column index. 0 indicates that
52	        % the HARQ process does not have any retransmission requirement.
53	        RetransmissionContextDL
54	
55	        %RetransmissionContextUL Information about uplink retransmission requirements
56	        % 1-by-P matrix where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. It stores the
57	        % information of HARQ processes for which the reception failed at gNB. This
58	        % information is used for assigning uplink grants for retransmissions. 1
59	        % indicates that the reception has failed for this particular HARQ process
60	        % ID governed by the column index. 0 indicates that the HARQ process does
61	        % not have any retransmission requirement.
62	        RetransmissionContextUL
63	
64	        %HarqProcessesDL Downlink HARQ processes context
65	        % 1-by-P structure array where 'P' is the number of DL HARQ
66	        % processes. Value at index 'i' contains the HARQ context of
67	        % process with HARQ ID 'i-1'
68	        HarqProcessesDL
69	
70	        %HarqProcessesUL Uplink HARQ processes context
71	        % 1-by-P structure array where 'P' is the number of UL HARQ
72	        % processes. Value at index 'i' contains the HARQ context of
73	        % process with HARQ ID 'i-1'
74	        HarqProcessesUL
75	
76	        %TBSizeDL Stores the size of transport block sent for DL HARQ processes
77	        % 1-by-P matrix where P is number of HARQ process. Value at index j stores
78	        % size of transport block sent for HARQ process index 'j'. Value is
79	        % 0 if DL HARQ process is free
80	        TBSizeDL
81	
82	        %TBSizeUL Stores the size of transport block to be received for UL HARQ processes
83	        % 1-by-P matrix where P is number of HARQ process. Value at index j stores
84	        % size of transport block to be received from UE for HARQ process index
85	        % 'j'. Value is 0, if no UL packet is expected for HARQ process of the UE
86	        TBSizeUL
87	
88	        %HarqNDIDL Last sent NDI value for the DL HARQ processes
89	        % 1-by-P logical array where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. Values at
90	        % index j stores the last sent NDI for the DL HARQ process index 'j'
91	        HarqNDIDL
92	
93	        %HarqNDIUL Last sent NDI value for the UL HARQ processes
94	        % 1-by-P logical array where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. Values at
95	        % index j stores the last sent NDI for the UL HARQ process index 'j'
96	        HarqNDIUL
97	
98	        %HarqStatusDL Status (free or busy) of each downlink HARQ process
99	        % 1-by-P cell array where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. A
100	        % non-empty value at index j indicates that HARQ process is busy with value
101	        % being the downlink assignment for the UE with HARQ index 'j'. Empty value
102	        % indicates that the HARQ process is free.
103	        HarqStatusDL
104	
105	        %HarqStatusUL Status (free or busy) of each uplink HARQ process
106	        % 1-by-P cell array where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. A non-empty
107	        % value at index j indicates that HARQ process is busy with value being the
108	        % uplink grant for the UE with HARQ index 'j'. Empty value indicates that
109	        % the HARQ process is free.
110	        HarqStatusUL
111	
112	        %IsBusyHARQDL Status (free or busy) of each downlink HARQ process of the UE
113	        % 1-by-P matrix where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. 1 indicates busy
114	        % HARQ process and 0 indicates that the HARQ process is free.
115	        IsBusyHARQDL
116	
117	        %IsBusyHARQUL Status (free or busy) of each uplink HARQ process
118	        % 1-by-P matrix where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. 1 indicates busy
119	        % HARQ process and 0 indicates that the HARQ process is free.
120	        IsBusyHARQUL
121	
122	        %RBGSize Size of a resource block group (RBG) in terms of number of RBs
123	        RBGSize
124	
125	        %NumRBGs Number of RBGs in the bandwidth
126	        NumRBGs
127	
128	        %MCSOffset Specifies the current MCS offset applied to the PXSCH transmissions.
129	        % It is a 1-by-2 matrix with corresponding MCS offsets for
130	        %DL/UL transmissions.
131	        MCSOffset
132	
133	        %XOverheadPDSCH Additional overheads in PDSCH transmission
134	        XOverheadPDSCH = 0;
135	
136	        %PrecodingGranularity PDSCH precoding granularity in terms of physical resource blocks (PRBs)
137	        PrecodingGranularity
138	    end
139	
140	    methods(Hidden)
141	        function obj = nrComponentCarrierContext(param, cellConfig, schedulerConfig)
142	            %nrComponentCarrierContext Initialize carrier context
143	            %
144	            % PARAM is a structure including the following fields:
145	            % NumTransmitAntennas          - Number of UE Tx antennas
146	            % NumReceiveAntennas           - Number of UE Rx antennas
147	            % CSIRSConfiguration           - CSI-RS configuration of the UE corresponding to PrimaryCarrierIndex
148	            % SRSConfiguration             - SRS configuration of the UE corresponding to PrimaryCarrierIndex
149	            % NumHARQ                      - Number of HARQ processes
150	            % RBGSizeConfig                - RBG configuration to derive number of RBs in an RBG
151	            % InitialCQIDL                 - Initial DL CQI
152	            % InitialMCSIndexUL            - Initial UL MCS
153	            % InitialMCSOffsetDL           - Initial MCS offset (link adaptation) for DL direction
154	            % InitialMCSOffsetUL           - Initial MCS offset (link adaptation) for UL direction
155	
156	            % CELLCONFIG is cell configuration.
157	            % SCHEDULERCONFIG is scheduler configuration.
158	
159	            if ~isempty(param)
160	                % Initialize the properties
161	                inputParam = ["NumTransmitAntennas", "NumReceiveAntennas", "CSIRSConfiguration", "SRSConfiguration"];
162	                for idx=1:numel(inputParam)
163	                    obj.(inputParam(idx)) = param.(inputParam(idx));
164	                end
165	                numHARQ = param.NumHARQ;
166	                % Create retransmission context
167	                obj.HarqStatusDL = cell(1, numHARQ);
168	                obj.HarqStatusUL = cell(1, numHARQ);
169	                obj.RetransmissionContextDL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
170	                obj.RetransmissionContextUL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
171	                obj.IsBusyHARQDL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
172	                obj.IsBusyHARQUL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
173	                obj.HarqNDIDL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
174	                obj.HarqNDIUL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
175	                obj.TBSizeDL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
176	                obj.TBSizeUL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
177	                ncw = 1; % Only single codeword
178	                % Create HARQ processes context array
179	                obj.HarqProcessesDL = nr5g.internal.nrNewHARQProcesses(numHARQ, schedulerConfig.RVSequence, ncw);
180	                obj.HarqProcessesUL = nr5g.internal.nrNewHARQProcesses(numHARQ, schedulerConfig.RVSequence, ncw);
181	
182	                % RBGSize configuration as 1 (configuration-1 RBG table) or 2
183	                % (configuration-2 RBG table) as defined in 3GPP TS 38.214 Section
184	                % 5.1.2.2.1. It defines the number of RBs in an RBG.
185	                nominalRBGSizePerBW = nr5g.internal.MACConstants.NominalRBGSizePerBW;
186	                rbgSizeIndex = min(find(cellConfig.NumResourceBlocks <= nominalRBGSizePerBW(:, 1), 1));
187	                if param.RBGSizeConfig == 1
188	                    obj.RBGSize = nominalRBGSizePerBW(rbgSizeIndex, 2);
189	                else % RBGSizeConfig is 2
190	                    obj.RBGSize = nominalRBGSizePerBW(rbgSizeIndex, 3);
191	                end
192	                obj.NumRBGs = ceil(cellConfig.NumResourceBlocks/obj.RBGSize);
193	
194	                % Set PDSCH overhead
195	                if ~isempty(param.CSIRSConfiguration)
196	                    obj.XOverheadPDSCH = 18;
197	                    % ThangTQ23_128T128R_Rel19: for array CSIRSConfiguration (128T uses
198	                    % 4Г—32-port resources), sum all port counts to get total antenna ports.
199	                    numCSIRSPorts = sum([obj.CSIRSConfiguration.NumCSIRSPorts]);
200	                else
201	                    numCSIRSPorts = cellConfig.NumTransmitAntennas;
202	                end
203	
204	                % ThangTQ23_128T128R_Rel19 Phase 4/4b: PrecodingGranularity depends on
205	                % whether subband PMI is active. Use CSIReportConfiguration.PMIMode
206	                % (set by nrNodeValidation from the pmiCQIMode flag) so wideband and
207	                % subband modes are both supported without hardcoding.
208	                useSubbandPRG = isfield(param, 'CSIReportConfiguration') && ...
209	                                ~isempty(param.CSIReportConfiguration) && ...
210	                                isfield(param.CSIReportConfiguration, 'PMIMode') && ...
211	                                strcmpi(param.CSIReportConfiguration.PMIMode, 'Subband');
212	                if useSubbandPRG
213	                    obj.PrecodingGranularity = 4; % PMI subband size = 4 RBs (TS 38.214 Table 5.2.1.4-2, NRBв€€[24,72])
214	                else
215	                    obj.PrecodingGranularity = cellConfig.NumResourceBlocks; % wideband: 1 PRG = full BW
216	                end
217	                % CSI measurements initialization (DL and UL)
218	                initialRank = 1; % Initial ranks for UEs
219	                wp = ones(numCSIRSPorts, 1)./sqrt(numCSIRSPorts);
220	                csirs = struct('RI', initialRank, 'PMISet', struct('i1',[1 1 1]), ...
221	                    'CQI', param.InitialCQIDL, 'W', wp);
222	                obj.CSIMeasurementDL = struct('CSIRS', csirs, 'SRS', []);
223	                obj.CSIMeasurementUL = struct('RI', initialRank, 'TPMI', 0, 'MCSIndex', param.InitialMCSIndexUL);
224	
225	                % Initialize link adaptation MCS offset values for the carrier. The
226	                % first column contains MCS offset in DL direction and second column
227	                % contains MCS offset in UL direction.
228	                obj.MCSOffset = [0 0];
229	                if ~isempty(schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigDL)
230	                    obj.MCSOffset(1) = param.InitialMCSOffsetDL;
231	                end
232	                if ~isempty(schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigUL)
233	                    obj.MCSOffset(2) = param.InitialMCSOffsetUL;
234	                end
235	            end
236	        end
237	
238	        function updateChannelQualityDL(obj, channelQualityInfo, schedulerConfig)
239	            %updateChannelQualityDL Update downlink channel quality information for a UE
240	            %   UPDATECHANNELQUALITYDL(OBJ, CHANNELQUALITYINFO) updates
241	            %   downlink (DL) channel quality information for a UE.
242	            %   CHANNELQUALITYINFO is a structure with these fields: RI, PMISet, W, CQI.
243	            %   SCHEDULERCONFIG is a structure containing field 'LinkAdaptationConfigDL'.
244	            %       LinkAdaptationConfigDL - Link adaptation (LA) configuration structure for downlink transmissions.
245	            %                          The structure contains these fields: InitialOffset, StepUp and StepDown
246	
247	            if schedulerConfig.CSIMeasurementSignalDLType && isfield(channelQualityInfo, 'SRSBasedDLMeasurements')
248	                % Update CSI measurement based on SRS
249	                obj.CSIMeasurementDL.SRS = channelQualityInfo.SRSBasedDLMeasurements;
250	            else
251	                % Update CSI measurement based on CSI-RS
252	                obj.CSIMeasurementDL.CSIRS.CQI = channelQualityInfo.CQI;
253	                obj.CSIMeasurementDL.CSIRS.RI = channelQualityInfo.RankIndicator;
254	                obj.CSIMeasurementDL.CSIRS.PMISet.i1 = channelQualityInfo.PMISet.i1;
255	                obj.CSIMeasurementDL.CSIRS.W = channelQualityInfo.W;
256	            end
257	            if ~isempty(schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigDL)
258	                % Reset MCS offset to InitialOffset on each CSI report
259	                % (by design per getMCSIndexOffset step 3 вЂ” each CSI period
260	                % is an independent OLLA window with fresh CQI measurements).
261	                obj.MCSOffset(1) = schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigDL.InitialOffset;
262	            end
263	        end
264	
265	        function updateChannelQualityUL(obj, channelQualityInfo, schedulerConfig)
266	            %updateChannelQualityUL Update uplink channel quality information for a UE
267	            %   UPDATECHANNELQUALITYUL(OBJ, CHANNELQUALITYINFO) updates
268	            %   uplink (UL) channel quality information for a UE.
269	            %   CHANNELQUALITYINFO is a structure with these fields: RI, TPMI, MCSIndex.
270	            %   SCHEDULERCONFIG is a structure containing field 'LinkAdaptationConfigUL'.
271	            %       LinkAdaptationConfigUL - Link adaptation (LA) configuration structure for uplink transmissions.
272	            %                          The structure contains these fields: InitialOffset, StepUp and StepDow
273	
274	            % Update CSI measurement
275	            obj.CSIMeasurementUL.MCSIndex = channelQualityInfo.MCSIndex;
276	            obj.CSIMeasurementUL.RI = channelQualityInfo.RankIndicator;
277	            obj.CSIMeasurementUL.TPMI = channelQualityInfo.TPMI;
278	            if ~isempty(schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigUL)
279	                % Reset link adaptation MCS offset value to initial offset
280	                obj.MCSOffset(2) = schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigUL.InitialOffset;
281	            end
282	        end
283	
284	        function updateSRSPeriod(obj, srsPeriod)
285	            %updateSRSPeriod Update the SRS periodicity of the UE for the specified carrier
286	
287	            obj.SRSConfiguration.SRSPeriod = srsPeriod;
288	        end
289	    end
290	end

classdef nrComponentCarrierContext < handle
    %nrComponentCarrierContext Carrier-specific information of a UE at gNB scheduler
    %
    %   This class reprsents the information which scheduler maintains for a
    %   connected UE on a particular carrier.
    %
    %   Note: This is an internal undocumented class and its API and/or
    %   functionality may change in subsequent releases.

    %   Copyright 2024 The MathWorks, Inc.

    properties(SetAccess=private)
        %NumTransmitAntennas Number of transmit antennas at the UE
        NumTransmitAntennas

        %NumReceiveAntennas Number of receive antennas at the UE
        NumReceiveAntennas

        %CSIRSConfiguration CSI-RS configuration of the UE
        CSIRSConfiguration

        %SRSConfiguration SRS configuration of the UE
        SRSConfiguration

        %CSIMeasurementDL Wideband DL channel measurement for the UE node based on CSI-RS and SRS
        % It is specified as a structure with these fields.
        %
        % CSIRS вЂ” A structure with these fields:
        %      RI вЂ” Rank indicator
        %      PMISet вЂ” Precoder matrix indicator (PMI) set
        %      CQI вЂ” Channel quality indicator
        %      W вЂ” Precoding matrix
        %
        % SRS вЂ” A structure with these fields:
        %       RI вЂ” Rank indicator
        %       W вЂ” Precoding matrix
        %       MCSIndex вЂ” Modulation and coding scheme index
        CSIMeasurementDL

        %CSIMeasurementUL UL CSI measurements based on SRS configured
        % It is a structure with the fields: 'RI', 'TPMI', 'MCSIndex'
        CSIMeasurementUL
    end

    properties(Hidden)
        %RetransmissionContextDL Information about downlink retransmission requirements
        % 1-by-P matrix where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. It stores the
        % information of HARQ processes for which the reception failed at gNB. This
        % information is used for assigning downlink assignments for
        % retransmissions. 1 indicates that the reception has failed for this
        % particular HARQ process ID governed by the column index. 0 indicates that
        % the HARQ process does not have any retransmission requirement.
        RetransmissionContextDL

        %RetransmissionContextUL Information about uplink retransmission requirements
        % 1-by-P matrix where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. It stores the
        % information of HARQ processes for which the reception failed at gNB. This
        % information is used for assigning uplink grants for retransmissions. 1
        % indicates that the reception has failed for this particular HARQ process
        % ID governed by the column index. 0 indicates that the HARQ process does
        % not have any retransmission requirement.
        RetransmissionContextUL

        %HarqProcessesDL Downlink HARQ processes context
        % 1-by-P structure array where 'P' is the number of DL HARQ
        % processes. Value at index 'i' contains the HARQ context of
        % process with HARQ ID 'i-1'
        HarqProcessesDL

        %HarqProcessesUL Uplink HARQ processes context
        % 1-by-P structure array where 'P' is the number of UL HARQ
        % processes. Value at index 'i' contains the HARQ context of
        % process with HARQ ID 'i-1'
        HarqProcessesUL

        %TBSizeDL Stores the size of transport block sent for DL HARQ processes
        % 1-by-P matrix where P is number of HARQ process. Value at index j stores
        % size of transport block sent for HARQ process index 'j'. Value is
        % 0 if DL HARQ process is free
        TBSizeDL

        %TBSizeUL Stores the size of transport block to be received for UL HARQ processes
        % 1-by-P matrix where P is number of HARQ process. Value at index j stores
        % size of transport block to be received from UE for HARQ process index
        % 'j'. Value is 0, if no UL packet is expected for HARQ process of the UE
        TBSizeUL

        %HarqNDIDL Last sent NDI value for the DL HARQ processes
        % 1-by-P logical array where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. Values at
        % index j stores the last sent NDI for the DL HARQ process index 'j'
        HarqNDIDL

        %HarqNDIUL Last sent NDI value for the UL HARQ processes
        % 1-by-P logical array where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. Values at
        % index j stores the last sent NDI for the UL HARQ process index 'j'
        HarqNDIUL

        %HarqStatusDL Status (free or busy) of each downlink HARQ process
        % 1-by-P cell array where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. A
        % non-empty value at index j indicates that HARQ process is busy with value
        % being the downlink assignment for the UE with HARQ index 'j'. Empty value
        % indicates that the HARQ process is free.
        HarqStatusDL

        %HarqStatusUL Status (free or busy) of each uplink HARQ process
        % 1-by-P cell array where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. A non-empty
        % value at index j indicates that HARQ process is busy with value being the
        % uplink grant for the UE with HARQ index 'j'. Empty value indicates that
        % the HARQ process is free.
        HarqStatusUL

        %IsBusyHARQDL Status (free or busy) of each downlink HARQ process of the UE
        % 1-by-P matrix where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. 1 indicates busy
        % HARQ process and 0 indicates that the HARQ process is free.
        IsBusyHARQDL

        %IsBusyHARQUL Status (free or busy) of each uplink HARQ process
        % 1-by-P matrix where 'P' is the number of HARQ processes. 1 indicates busy
        % HARQ process and 0 indicates that the HARQ process is free.
        IsBusyHARQUL

        %RBGSize Size of a resource block group (RBG) in terms of number of RBs
        RBGSize

        %NumRBGs Number of RBGs in the bandwidth
        NumRBGs

        %MCSOffset Specifies the current MCS offset applied to the PXSCH transmissions.
        % It is a 1-by-2 matrix with corresponding MCS offsets for
        %DL/UL transmissions.
        MCSOffset

        %XOverheadPDSCH Additional overheads in PDSCH transmission
        XOverheadPDSCH = 0;

        %PrecodingGranularity PDSCH precoding granularity in terms of physical resource blocks (PRBs)
        PrecodingGranularity
    end

    methods(Hidden)
        function obj = nrComponentCarrierContext(param, cellConfig, schedulerConfig)
            %nrComponentCarrierContext Initialize carrier context
            %
            % PARAM is a structure including the following fields:
            % NumTransmitAntennas          - Number of UE Tx antennas
            % NumReceiveAntennas           - Number of UE Rx antennas
            % CSIRSConfiguration           - CSI-RS configuration of the UE corresponding to PrimaryCarrierIndex
            % SRSConfiguration             - SRS configuration of the UE corresponding to PrimaryCarrierIndex
            % NumHARQ                      - Number of HARQ processes
            % RBGSizeConfig                - RBG configuration to derive number of RBs in an RBG
            % InitialCQIDL                 - Initial DL CQI
            % InitialMCSIndexUL            - Initial UL MCS
            % InitialMCSOffsetDL           - Initial MCS offset (link adaptation) for DL direction
            % InitialMCSOffsetUL           - Initial MCS offset (link adaptation) for UL direction

            % CELLCONFIG is cell configuration.
            % SCHEDULERCONFIG is scheduler configuration.

            if ~isempty(param)
                % Initialize the properties
                inputParam = ["NumTransmitAntennas", "NumReceiveAntennas", "CSIRSConfiguration", "SRSConfiguration"];
                for idx=1:numel(inputParam)
                    obj.(inputParam(idx)) = param.(inputParam(idx));
                end
                numHARQ = param.NumHARQ;
                % Create retransmission context
                obj.HarqStatusDL = cell(1, numHARQ);
                obj.HarqStatusUL = cell(1, numHARQ);
                obj.RetransmissionContextDL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
                obj.RetransmissionContextUL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
                obj.IsBusyHARQDL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
                obj.IsBusyHARQUL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
                obj.HarqNDIDL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
                obj.HarqNDIUL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
                obj.TBSizeDL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
                obj.TBSizeUL = zeros(1, numHARQ);
                ncw = 1; % Only single codeword
                % Create HARQ processes context array
                obj.HarqProcessesDL = nr5g.internal.nrNewHARQProcesses(numHARQ, schedulerConfig.RVSequence, ncw);
                obj.HarqProcessesUL = nr5g.internal.nrNewHARQProcesses(numHARQ, schedulerConfig.RVSequence, ncw);

                % RBGSize configuration as 1 (configuration-1 RBG table) or 2
                % (configuration-2 RBG table) as defined in 3GPP TS 38.214 Section
                % 5.1.2.2.1. It defines the number of RBs in an RBG.
                nominalRBGSizePerBW = nr5g.internal.MACConstants.NominalRBGSizePerBW;
                rbgSizeIndex = min(find(cellConfig.NumResourceBlocks <= nominalRBGSizePerBW(:, 1), 1));
                if param.RBGSizeConfig == 1
                    obj.RBGSize = nominalRBGSizePerBW(rbgSizeIndex, 2);
                else % RBGSizeConfig is 2
                    obj.RBGSize = nominalRBGSizePerBW(rbgSizeIndex, 3);
                end
                obj.NumRBGs = ceil(cellConfig.NumResourceBlocks/obj.RBGSize);

                % Set PDSCH overhead
                if ~isempty(param.CSIRSConfiguration)
                    obj.XOverheadPDSCH = 18;
                    % ThangTQ23_128T128R_Rel19: for array CSIRSConfiguration (128T uses
                    % 4Г—32-port resources), sum all port counts to get total antenna ports.
                    numCSIRSPorts = sum([obj.CSIRSConfiguration.NumCSIRSPorts]);
                else
                    numCSIRSPorts = cellConfig.NumTransmitAntennas;
                end

                % ThangTQ23_128T128R_Rel19 Phase 4/4b: PrecodingGranularity depends on
                % whether subband PMI is active. Use CSIReportConfiguration.PMIMode
                % (set by nrNodeValidation from the pmiCQIMode flag) so wideband and
                % subband modes are both supported without hardcoding.
                useSubbandPRG = isfield(param, 'CSIReportConfiguration') && ...
                                ~isempty(param.CSIReportConfiguration) && ...
                                isfield(param.CSIReportConfiguration, 'PMIMode') && ...
                                strcmpi(param.CSIReportConfiguration.PMIMode, 'Subband');
                if useSubbandPRG
                    obj.PrecodingGranularity = 4; % PMI subband size = 4 RBs (TS 38.214 Table 5.2.1.4-2, NRBв€€[24,72])
                else
                    obj.PrecodingGranularity = cellConfig.NumResourceBlocks; % wideband: 1 PRG = full BW
                end
                % CSI measurements initialization (DL and UL)
                initialRank = 1; % Initial ranks for UEs
                wp = ones(numCSIRSPorts, 1)./sqrt(numCSIRSPorts);
                csirs = struct('RI', initialRank, 'PMISet', struct('i1',[1 1 1]), ...
                    'CQI', param.InitialCQIDL, 'W', wp);
                obj.CSIMeasurementDL = struct('CSIRS', csirs, 'SRS', []);
                obj.CSIMeasurementUL = struct('RI', initialRank, 'TPMI', 0, 'MCSIndex', param.InitialMCSIndexUL);

                % Initialize link adaptation MCS offset values for the carrier. The
                % first column contains MCS offset in DL direction and second column
                % contains MCS offset in UL direction.
                obj.MCSOffset = [0 0];
                if ~isempty(schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigDL)
                    obj.MCSOffset(1) = param.InitialMCSOffsetDL;
                end
                if ~isempty(schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigUL)
                    obj.MCSOffset(2) = param.InitialMCSOffsetUL;
                end
            end
        end

        function updateChannelQualityDL(obj, channelQualityInfo, schedulerConfig)
            %updateChannelQualityDL Update downlink channel quality information for a UE
            %   UPDATECHANNELQUALITYDL(OBJ, CHANNELQUALITYINFO) updates
            %   downlink (DL) channel quality information for a UE.
            %   CHANNELQUALITYINFO is a structure with these fields: RI, PMISet, W, CQI.
            %   SCHEDULERCONFIG is a structure containing field 'LinkAdaptationConfigDL'.
            %       LinkAdaptationConfigDL - Link adaptation (LA) configuration structure for downlink transmissions.
            %                          The structure contains these fields: InitialOffset, StepUp and StepDown

            if schedulerConfig.CSIMeasurementSignalDLType && isfield(channelQualityInfo, 'SRSBasedDLMeasurements')
                % Update CSI measurement based on SRS
                obj.CSIMeasurementDL.SRS = channelQualityInfo.SRSBasedDLMeasurements;
            else
                % Update CSI measurement based on CSI-RS
                obj.CSIMeasurementDL.CSIRS.CQI = channelQualityInfo.CQI;
                obj.CSIMeasurementDL.CSIRS.RI = channelQualityInfo.RankIndicator;
                obj.CSIMeasurementDL.CSIRS.PMISet.i1 = channelQualityInfo.PMISet.i1;
                obj.CSIMeasurementDL.CSIRS.W = channelQualityInfo.W;
            end
            if ~isempty(schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigDL)
                % Reset MCS offset to InitialOffset on each CSI report
                % (by design per getMCSIndexOffset step 3 вЂ” each CSI period
                % is an independent OLLA window with fresh CQI measurements).
                obj.MCSOffset(1) = schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigDL.InitialOffset;
            end
        end

        function updateChannelQualityUL(obj, channelQualityInfo, schedulerConfig)
            %updateChannelQualityUL Update uplink channel quality information for a UE
            %   UPDATECHANNELQUALITYUL(OBJ, CHANNELQUALITYINFO) updates
            %   uplink (UL) channel quality information for a UE.
            %   CHANNELQUALITYINFO is a structure with these fields: RI, TPMI, MCSIndex.
            %   SCHEDULERCONFIG is a structure containing field 'LinkAdaptationConfigUL'.
            %       LinkAdaptationConfigUL - Link adaptation (LA) configuration structure for uplink transmissions.
            %                          The structure contains these fields: InitialOffset, StepUp and StepDow

            % Update CSI measurement
            obj.CSIMeasurementUL.MCSIndex = channelQualityInfo.MCSIndex;
            obj.CSIMeasurementUL.RI = channelQualityInfo.RankIndicator;
            obj.CSIMeasurementUL.TPMI = channelQualityInfo.TPMI;
            if ~isempty(schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigUL)
                % Reset link adaptation MCS offset value to initial offset
                obj.MCSOffset(2) = schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigUL.InitialOffset;
            end
        end

        function updateSRSPeriod(obj, srsPeriod)
            %updateSRSPeriod Update the SRS periodicity of the UE for the specified carrier

            obj.SRSConfiguration.SRSPeriod = srsPeriod;
        end
    end
end

function mumimoUEs = nrExtractMUMIMOUserlist(muMIMOConfigDL, schedulerInput, mcsInfo, isSRSApplicable)
%extractMUMIMOUserlist Extract the MU-MIMO candidate UEs
%
%   Note: This is an internal undocumented function and its API and/or
%   functionality may change in subsequent releases.
%
%   MUMIMOUES = nr5g.internal.nrExtractMUMIMOUserlist(MUMIMOCONFIGDL,...
%   SCHEDULERINPUT, MCSINFO, ISSRSAPPLICABLE) returns a logical array
%   indicating MU-MIMO capable UEs those meet the contraints specified in
%   the MUMIMOCONFIGDL structure. i.e., UEs with non-zero buffer and meets
%   MinNumRBs, MinCQI for CSI-RS-based downlink MU-MIMO and MinSINR
%   for SRS-based downlink MU-MIMO.
%
%   MUMIMOCONFIGDL structure contains the contraints that the user pairing
%   algorithm needs for determining the MU-MIMO candidacy among UEs.
%
%   SCHEDULERINPUT structure contains the fields which scheduler
%   needs for selecting the UE for resource allocation.
%
%   MCSINFO is the MCS table used for downlink. It contains the mapping of
%   MCS indices with Modulation and Coding schemes.
%
%   ISSRSAPPLICABLE is the flag used to indicate if the CSI measurement
%   type is SRS, where a logical 'true' represents the SRS is applicable.
%
%   MUMIMOUES is a logical array and determines if the UE is
%   eligible for MU-MIMO pairing, where a logical 'true' represents
%   the corresponding UE is MU-MIMO capable.

%   Copyright 2024 The MathWorks, Inc.

mumimoUEs = zeros(size(schedulerInput.eligibleUEs, 2), 1);
mcs = schedulerInput.mcsRBG(:, 1)';
nPRB = muMIMOConfigDL.MinNumRBs;
nREPerPRB = 12*schedulerInput.numSym;

% Find MU-MIMO capable UEs
for index = 1:size(schedulerInput.eligibleUEs, 2)
    % Calculate served bits for number of RBs equal to MinNumRBs
    nlayers = schedulerInput.selectedRank(index);
    infoMCS = mcsInfo(mcs(index) + 1, :);
    modSchemeBits = infoMCS(1); % Bits per symbol for modulation scheme
    modScheme = nr5g.internal.getModulationScheme(modSchemeBits);
    codeRate = infoMCS(2)/1024;
    servedBits = nrTBS(modScheme, nlayers, nPRB, nREPerPRB, codeRate);

    % MU-MIMO candidacy
    if(schedulerInput.bufferStatus(index) > servedBits)
        %Apply filtering rule
        if ~isSRSApplicable
            cqi = schedulerInput.cqiRBG(:, 1)';
            if (cqi(index) >= muMIMOConfigDL.MinCQI)
                % logical Array
                mumimoUEs(index) = 1;
            end
        else
            if ~isempty(schedulerInput.SINRs{index}) && (schedulerInput.SINRs{index} >= muMIMOConfigDL.MinSINR)
                % logical Array
                mumimoUEs(index) = 1;
            end
        end
    end
end
end

content.thinking:
Now I have a complete picture. Let me summarize what I found:

1. **Subband CQI is present**: Yes. When `pmiCQIMode = "Subband"`, `CSIMeasurementDL.CSIRS.CQI` is a `[1Г—numRBs]` vector (subband CQI per RB).

2. **How channelQuality matrix is built** (lines 340-350): The code does expand the subband CQI correctly - if `CQI` is a vector (subband mode), it stores it directly as `channelQuality(rnti, :)` - a `[numUEs Г— numRBs]` matrix.
   
3. **MCS calculation for RB requirement** (line 2671): `calculateRBRequirement` only uses `CQI(1)` - the first subband CQI element - for determining how many RBs are needed, which is marked as "Assuming wideband CQI" and represents a suboptimal approach.

4. **BestCQI priority calculation**: The priority metric `uePriority(i) = instantaneousThroughput^AlphaPFS / averageDataRate^BetaPFS` relies on `bitsPerRB` from `calculateRBRequirement`, which in turn uses only that first CQI value, so the entire priority ranking is based on a single subband rather than leveraging the full subband information available.

5. **MCS per RBG in scheduling** (lines 1452-1455): When assigning RBGs in RAT-0, the code correctly extracts the actual subband CQI for each RBG range, then averages across RBs within that RBG to compute the MCS - this part is handling per-RBG CQI properly.

6. **Final MCS for DL grants** (lines 1547-1551): After allocation, the scheduler retrieves the CQI values for all assigned RBs and computes their mean to determine the final MCS for the downlink grant.

7. **MU-MIMO MinCQI filter** (line 51 in nrExtractMUMIMOUserlist.m): The MU-MIMO user selection only looks at the first RB's CQI within each RBG for the minimum CQI check, which is a bit simplistic but workable.

The key finding is that subband CQI is actually present and being used during scheduling - the RAT-0 allocator applies per-RBG CQI when assigning RBGs and then uses the average CQI across the final allocated RBs for the MCS calculation, though some components like the MU-MIMO filter could be more thorough in how they leverage the full subband information.

However, there's a critical limitation: the pre-scheduling phase that determines priorities and estimates RB requirements still relies only on the first subband's CQI value, which means the BestCQI priority metric and the RB estimation for proportional fair scheduling don't fully account for the actual channel quality distribution across the band.

The subband CQI gets used later for MCS calculation, but the actual UE selection for each RBG is purely based on this round-robin ordering through the pre-sorted eligible UEs.

The scheduler is doing frequency-flat UE selection based on wideband priority, then applying per-RBG CQI only when calculating MCS and determining MU-MIMO pairing.

content.thinking:
The user is asking whether it's worth implementing frequency-selective scheduling (selecting the UE with the best CQI for each specific subband/RBG). Let me think about this carefully in the context of their simulation setup.

Key parameters:
- numRB = 24 (10 MHz, 30 kHz SCS)
- RBG size = 4 (from code: since numRB в€€ [24,72], sbSize = 4)
- numSubbands = ceil(24/4) = 6 subbands
- Channel: CDL-B, 100ns delay spread, 5Hz Doppler


- 64 UEs with MU-MIMO up to 12 paired users and 128T128R massive MIMO

The tradeoffs are pretty clear: with only 6 RBGs across 10 MHz, the frequency diversity gains from subband-level scheduling are limited, and CDL-B's moderate frequency selectivity means the coherence bandwidth spans multiple RBs, so there's not much variation in channel quality across the few subbands available.

The real advantage here comes from spatial multiplexing with the massive antenna array, not frequency diversity. With 64 UEs competing for just 6 RBGs, multiuser diversity from selecting the best UE per RBG would normally be significant, but the constraint is tight enough that each UE gets only a handful of resources anyway.

The precoder mismatch isn't actually a problem since each subband already has its own PMI, so assigning a UE based on best CQI naturally pairs with the right precoder. But adding frequency-selective scheduling on top of MU-MIMO pairing creates a joint optimization challenge вЂ” I'd need to find not just the best UE per RBG, but also compatible pairing partners, which gets complex quickly. The implementation would shift from simple priority-based selection to searching for maximum CQI per RBG, and whether that complexity pays off depends on how much the CQI profiles actually vary across subbands.

Given only 6 subbands and the 10 MHz CDL-B channel, the frequency selectivity gains would be relatively small compared to what MU-MIMO spatial multiplexing already provides. In a wider 100 MHz system, this approach would be much more compelling, but here the added complexity probably isn't justified.

The user has a very powerful machine:
- Intel i9-14900 (32 cores/threads) @ 5.5 GHz
- NVIDIA RTX 4000 Ada Generation
- 64 GB RAM
- Ubuntu 24.04

They want to speed up the MATLAB simulation. Let me think about what approaches are available:

1. **MATLAB Parallel Computing Toolbox** - parfor loops, parallel pool
2. **Increase bandwidth to 100 MHz** - they mentioned this is for fast simulation
3. **Reduce simulation parameters** - fewer frames, fewer UEs
4. **MATLAB GPU computing** - for numerical operations


5. **Profile the code** to identify where time is actually being spent

For their specific setup with 64 UEs, 128T128R antenna arrays, CDL-B channel model, and 50 frames, the main performance bottlenecks are likely the channel simulation itself, the CSI feedback computation for PMI and CQI per UE, and the scheduling logic. The `wirelessNetworkSimulator` runs sequentially by default, so the CSI-RS measurements and PMI calculations for each UE during each CSI period are probably where most of the computation time goes.

I can see several parallelization opportunities here. Each UE's CSI computation is independent, so that's a good candidate for `parfor`. They could also parallelize across multiple Monte Carlo simulation runs. With a 32-core system available, setting up a MATLAB parallel pool would help. Looking at the actual code, the CDL channel model creation and application, plus the eTypeII-r19 PMI computation for 128 transmit antennas, are the expensive operations. The most effective optimizations would be enabling the parallel pool for channel computations, using `parfor` in the CSI measurement loop where possible, and potentially reducing the number of UEs during initial testing to validate the approach. then scale up)
4. Use wideband mode for quick testing

Since this is using `wirelessNetworkSimulator` (a MATLAB toolbox), parallelizing the main loop directly is challenging. The real question is whether per-UE computations can be parallelized.

Looking at the code structure, the main bottlenecks are likely:
1. **CDL channel application** - per UE, per slot
2. **CSI feedback computation** - eTypeII-r19 PMI selection is expensive for 128 ports
3. **L2SM (Link-to-System Mapping)** - per allocation

For a 32-core machine, I'd start a MATLAB parallel pool and use `parfor` in channel simulation loops where feasible, potentially running multiple seeds in parallel.

But the `wirelessNetworkSimulator.run()` is fundamentally a sequential loop, so parallelization within a single run is limited. The more practical approach is to reduce parameters firstвЂ”cut numUEs to 16-32 and numFrameSimulation to 20-30вЂ”then use `parfor` across multiple seeds if doing Monte Carlo analysis. I should profile with `profile on` to identify the actual bottleneck before optimizing.

The RTX 4000 GPU won't help much since wirelessNetworkSimulator doesn't natively support GPU acceleration. My best actionable advice: profile first, then target the specific bottleneckвЂ”either simplify the channel model if CDL is the issue, or switch to wideband PMI computation for most runs and only use subband for final validation. For parallelism, run multiple independent simulations across workers.

Now I have a clear picture of the bottleneck. The per-subband CQI computation loop at lines 282-295 runs numSBs (= 6) L2SM calls per UE per CSI event. With 64 UEs, that's 384 L2SM calls per CSI period.

For i9-14900 with 32 threads, the key parallelization options are:

1. **parfor over subbands** (line 282): `for sb = 1:numSBs` в†’ `parfor sb = 1:numSBs`
   - But L2SM state needs to be handled carefully (value copy already done: `l2sm_sb = obj.L2SMCSI`)
   - This is the most accessible optimization

2. **parfor over UEs** in the CSI computation: each UE's CSI is independent, though `wirelessNetworkSimulator` drives UE objects sequentially, making this harder to implement

3. **Multiple parallel simulation runs** (parfor over seeds/trials) is the most practical approach for the main script

4. **The main bottleneck**: `nrDLPMISelect` (3443 lines) вЂ” this eTypeII-r19 PMI computation runs once per CSI event per UE, totaling 384 calls per CSI period across 6 subbands and 64 UEs

The subband CQI loop at line 282 is a candidate for parallelization since each subband's computation is independent and the outputs are indexed arrays, but this only parallelizes 6 iterations per call. Parallelizing over multiple seeds for statistical runs would be more effective overall.

The real performance bottleneck is likely the `nrDLPMISelect` function running the eTypeII-r19 codebook search вЂ” with 64 UEs and a CSI period of 10 slots, that's 64 PMI computations every 5ms of simulation time. I could reduce CSIReportPeriodicity to cut down on these computations, though that trades off accuracy for speed.

For practical optimization without major refactoring, the best approach is running multiple independent simulation seeds in parallel using parfor. I should profile the code first to confirm the bottleneck, then implement a few targeted improvements: switch to Wideband mode during development, parallelize the subband CQI loop in nrUEAbstractPHY.m, run independent realizations in parallel, and reduce the number of frames and UEs for prototyping to speed up iteration. the loop body only uses local variables and indexed output arrays, so converting to `parfor` should be safeвЂ”the main risk would be if those L2SM functions rely on hidden state, but the value copy pattern suggests they don't.

With just 6 iterations though, the parallelization overhead might outweigh the benefit unless each iteration is computationally expensive, which it likely is given the eTypeII-r19 128T complexity.

The real optimization opportunities are probably at a higher level: running multiple seeds in parallel would give clearer gains, and I should profile the code to pinpoint where the actual bottleneck is. The channel model application across UEs might also be a sequential bottleneck worth investigating.

So my recommendations would prioritize setting up a parallel pool with 32 workers and switching to wideband CQI mode for faster testing, then moving to parallelizing the subband loop in the PHY layer if needed, with GPU acceleration as a longer-term option if the channel estimation becomes the limiting factor.

The user is asking a conceptual question about what parallelizing over seeds actually means. They're saying: if you run parfor over different seeds, each run is just solving the same time-sequential simulation (slot 0 to slot N) but with a different random channel realization. It's not about speeding up a single simulation, but about getting multiple independent statistical samples.

The user seems to understand this correctly and is questioning whether this is truly useful or just for statistical averaging. Let me think about what they're really asking:

1. Yes, each parfor seed runs the SAME simulation from slot 0 to slot N with a different random channel
2. This doesn't make a single run faster - it's for Monte Carlo statistical significance


3. The user's intuition is right: parallelizing seeds doesn't accelerate the simulation itself, but running N seeds in parallel does give you N independent statistical samples in roughly the time of one run, which is valuable for getting robust performance metrics like CDFs and throughput curves.

BбєЎn hiб»ѓu Д‘Гєng hoГ n toГ n.

`parfor` over seeds **khГґng lГ m nhanh mб»™t simulation Д‘ЖЎn lбє»** вЂ” mб»—i worker vбє«n chбєЎy tuбє§n tб»± tб»« slot 0 Д‘бєїn slot cuб»‘i theo event-driven loop, chб»‰ vб»›i channel realization khГЎc nhau (do `rng(seed)` khГЎc nhau).

ДђГўy lГ  **Monte Carlo parallelism**, khГґng phбєЈi speedup:

```

Worker 2: slot0в†’slot1в†’...в†’slot1000  (seed=2, channel realization B)
...

```

content.thinking:
The user is observing that BLER is very high (0.35 - 0.59, so 35% to 59%). This is indeed very high - target should be around 10% with the OLLA configuration set up.

Let me look at the OLLA configuration and understand why BLER is so high.

From the main script:
```matlab
oLLAConfig = struct('InitialOffset', 0, 'StepUp', 0.27, 'StepDown', 0.03);
% Target BLER = StepDown/(StepDown+StepUp) = 0.03/0.30 = 10%


```

But BLER is 35-59%, which is way above the 10% target. I'm also noticing that MCS ranges from 0 to 27 for all UEs, SINR is very high at 31.9 dB for most subbands which should map to CQI=15, yet BLER is still 0.35-0.59. This is very suspicious - CQI=15 with that SINR should give excellent performance, not such high error rates. I'm wondering if there's an issue with the OLLA reset mechanism that might be interfering with the adaptation.

Looking at the OLLA logic more carefully, the offset increases by 0.27 on each NACK (which lowers the MCS) and decreases by 0.03 on each ACK. The target BLER is designed to be StepDown/(StepDown+StepUp) = 0.03/0.30 = 10%, but if the offset keeps resetting to zero every CSI period, the algorithm never gets a chance to converge and stabilize at the right MCS level.

```
The real issue is that CQI is reported based on single-user SINR measurements from the CSI-RS (31.9 dB), but in MU-MIMO with 12 paired UEs, the actual experienced SINR is much lower due to inter-user interference. So the scheduler picks a high MCS based on CQI=15, but the actual link performance is degraded by the interference from spatial multiplexing, causing the high BLER. The OLLA algorithm can't compensate fast enough because it's fighting against this fundamental mismatch between reported and actual channel conditions.
```

I'm checking the OLLA reset logic...

The code resets the MCS offset back to the initial value (0) on every CSI report, which means OLLA can't accumulate learning across periods вЂ” it has to ramp up from scratch each time. The design treats each CSI period as an independent window, so with a step size of 0.27, OLLA needs just a few NACKs to start backing off the MCS, but this happens within a very tight 10-slot window before resetting again.

Looking at the actual grant distribution in TDD, there might only be 5-6 downlink slots per CSI period, giving maybe 7-8 grants per UE. If all of those are NACKs, the offset climbs to about 1.89, which only decreases the MCS by 1 вЂ” nowhere near enough to fix a 50% block error rate when the CQI is already overestimating at 15.

The core issue is that CQI 15 maps to MCS 24 in single-user mode, but with 12 users in MU-MIMO, the actual SINR drops significantly. OLLA can't adapt fast enough since it resets every CSI period. I'm considering whether to increase the step-up rate, start with a negative offset to be more conservative, skip the OLLA reset on CSI updates, or add a MU-MIMO-specific back-off to the CQI itself.

I'm also noticing the MCS minimum is 0 in the summary, so OLLA is pushing down to the floor sometimes, yet the average stays at 22-24 with high variance. This suggests the fundamental problem might be deeper вЂ” possibly related to how the abstract PHY with 128T/128R is modeling the channel.

Looking at how interference is computed during CSI feedback, the system does account for inter-user interference from paired UEs through the interference preparation function, but there's a timing mismatch: the CSI-RS measurement happens before the actual MU-MIMO pairing is known, so the interference estimate during feedback doesn't match the real pairing interference that occurs during data transmission.

The core issue is that CQI feedback assumes single-user conditions while actual transmission experiences multi-user interference, which is a fundamental limitation in MU-MIMO simulations. The high BLER stems from three main factors: the CQI overestimation from single-user assumptions, OLLA resetting too frequently to adapt properly, and potentially aggressive user pairing with the semi-orthogonality factor allowing less-orthogonal users to be paired together. I should focus on practical solutions to address these.

1	function mcsOffset = getMCSIndexOffset(laConfig,offset,rxResult)
2	%getMCSIndexOffset returns the updated Modulation and Coding
3	% Scheme (MCS) offset.
4	%
5	%   Note: This is an internal undocumented function and its API and/or
6	%   functionality may change in subsequent releases
7	%
8	%   MCSOFFSET = nr5g.internal.getMCSIndexOffset(LACONFIG, OFFSET, RXRESULT)
9	%   returns the MCS offset upon receiving PDSCH or PUSCH feedback.
10	% 
11	%   MCSOFFSET           - Updated MCS offset after accounting for the
12	%                         downlink(DL) or uplink(UL) feedback
13	%   LACONFIG              - Link adaptation (LA) configuration structure
14	%                           for DL or UL transmissions.
15	%       InitialOffset     - Initial MCS offset applied to all UEs. This
16	%                           value factors in the channel measurements
17	%                           errors at the UE node. To determine the final
18	%                           MCS for the DL transmission, the scheduler
19	%                           applies this offset to the channel measurement
20	%                           based MCS. It is an integer in the range [-27,
21	%                           27]. The default value is 0.
22	%       StepUp            - Incremental value for the MCS offset when a
23	%                           packet reception fails. It is a numeric
24	%                           scalar in the range [0, 27]. The default value
25	%                           is 0.27.
26	%       StepDown          - Decremental value for the MCS offset when a
27	%                           packet reception is successful. It is a
28	%                           numeric scalar in the range [0, 27]. The
29	%                           default value is 0.03.
30	%   OFFSET              - Current MCS offset
31	%   RXRESULT            - Reception feedback where '1' means successful
32	%                         and '0' means failure
33	%
34	% The function implements the link adaptation algorithm outlined in
35	% reference [1], with a notable modification. In contrast to the original
36	% approach, which applies an offset to the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), the
37	% adopted method applies the offset directly to the MCS index.
38	% 
39	% The adopted link adaptation algorithm consists of these steps:
40	%   1. Define the LA configuration parameters StepUp and StepDown.
41	%      The target Block error rate, TargetBLER, is defined as StepDown /
42	%      (StepDown + StepUp).
43	%   2. Upon receiving channel measurements feedback (CQI) from the UE node,
44	%      the gNB maps the reported CQI to an appropriate MCS value. The MCS
45	%      table used for the DL LA complies with 3GPP TS 38.214, Table
46	%      5.1.3.1-2.
47	%   3. Reset MCSOffset to InitialOffset on each CSI reporting
48	%      periodicity, considering any channel measurement errors.
49	%   4. Determine the MCS for physical downlink shared channel (PDSCH)
50	%      transmission as the MCS reported by the UE node minus the MCS offset
51	%      (PDSCH_MCS = UEReportedMCS - MCSOffset)
52	%   5. If the UE reports a successful PDSCH reception, decrease the
53	%      MCSOffset by StepDown.
54	%   6. If the UE reports a failed PDSCH reception, increase the
55	%      MCSOffset by StepUp.
56	%   
57	% Similarly, for UL link adaptation, the gNB calculates the  UL MCS
58	% based on the sounding reference signal (SRS) channel measurements.
59	% Determine the physical uplink shared channel (PUSCH) as the MCS
60	% calculated by the gNB node minus the MCS offset (PUSCH_MCS =
61	% gNBMeasuredMCS - MCSOffset)
62	%   
63	% [1] M. G. Sarret, D. Catania, F. Frederiksen, A. F. Cattoni, G.
64	% Berardinelli, and P. Mogensen,  "Dynamic outer loop link adaptation for
65	% the 5G centimeterwave concept", in Proc. Eur. Wireless, 2015, pp. 1вЂ“6
66	
67	%   Copyright 2023 The MathWorks, Inc.
68	
69	
70	% As per 3GPP TS 38.214 - Table 5.1.3.1-2, '27' is the maximum
71	% configurable MCS index.
72	if rxResult == 1
73	    % Reception is successful
74	    mcsOffset = max(-27, offset-laConfig.StepDown);
75	else
76	    % Reception is failure 
77	    mcsOffset = min(27, offset+laConfig.StepUp);
78	end
79	end
80	
81

function mcsOffset = getMCSIndexOffset(laConfig,offset,rxResult)
%getMCSIndexOffset returns the updated Modulation and Coding
% Scheme (MCS) offset.
%
%   Note: This is an internal undocumented function and its API and/or
%   functionality may change in subsequent releases
%
%   MCSOFFSET = nr5g.internal.getMCSIndexOffset(LACONFIG, OFFSET, RXRESULT)
%   returns the MCS offset upon receiving PDSCH or PUSCH feedback.
% 
%   MCSOFFSET           - Updated MCS offset after accounting for the
%                         downlink(DL) or uplink(UL) feedback
%   LACONFIG              - Link adaptation (LA) configuration structure
%                           for DL or UL transmissions.
%       InitialOffset     - Initial MCS offset applied to all UEs. This
%                           value factors in the channel measurements
%                           errors at the UE node. To determine the final
%                           MCS for the DL transmission, the scheduler
%                           applies this offset to the channel measurement
%                           based MCS. It is an integer in the range [-27,
%                           27]. The default value is 0.
%       StepUp            - Incremental value for the MCS offset when a
%                           packet reception fails. It is a numeric
%                           scalar in the range [0, 27]. The default value
%                           is 0.27.
%       StepDown          - Decremental value for the MCS offset when a
%                           packet reception is successful. It is a
%                           numeric scalar in the range [0, 27]. The
%                           default value is 0.03.
%   OFFSET              - Current MCS offset
%   RXRESULT            - Reception feedback where '1' means successful
%                         and '0' means failure
%
% The function implements the link adaptation algorithm outlined in
% reference [1], with a notable modification. In contrast to the original
% approach, which applies an offset to the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), the
% adopted method applies the offset directly to the MCS index.
% 
% The adopted link adaptation algorithm consists of these steps:
%   1. Define the LA configuration parameters StepUp and StepDown.
%      The target Block error rate, TargetBLER, is defined as StepDown /
%      (StepDown + StepUp).
%   2. Upon receiving channel measurements feedback (CQI) from the UE node,
%      the gNB maps the reported CQI to an appropriate MCS value. The MCS
%      table used for the DL LA complies with 3GPP TS 38.214, Table
%      5.1.3.1-2.
%   3. Reset MCSOffset to InitialOffset on each CSI reporting
%      periodicity, considering any channel measurement errors.
%   4. Determine the MCS for physical downlink shared channel (PDSCH)
%      transmission as the MCS reported by the UE node minus the MCS offset
%      (PDSCH_MCS = UEReportedMCS - MCSOffset)
%   5. If the UE reports a successful PDSCH reception, decrease the
%      MCSOffset by StepDown.
%   6. If the UE reports a failed PDSCH reception, increase the
%      MCSOffset by StepUp.
%   
% Similarly, for UL link adaptation, the gNB calculates the  UL MCS
% based on the sounding reference signal (SRS) channel measurements.
% Determine the physical uplink shared channel (PUSCH) as the MCS
% calculated by the gNB node minus the MCS offset (PUSCH_MCS =
% gNBMeasuredMCS - MCSOffset)
%   
% [1] M. G. Sarret, D. Catania, F. Frederiksen, A. F. Cattoni, G.
% Berardinelli, and P. Mogensen,  "Dynamic outer loop link adaptation for
% the 5G centimeterwave concept", in Proc. Eur. Wireless, 2015, pp. 1вЂ“6

%   Copyright 2023 The MathWorks, Inc.


% As per 3GPP TS 38.214 - Table 5.1.3.1-2, '27' is the maximum
% configurable MCS index.
if rxResult == 1
    % Reception is successful
    mcsOffset = max(-27, offset-laConfig.StepDown);
else
    % Reception is failure 
    mcsOffset = min(27, offset+laConfig.StepUp);
end
end

input.description: Check if MU-MIMO interference is included in CQI computation

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture of the two root causes of high BLER:

## Root Cause 1: CQI is single-user (SU-MIMO), but actual transmission is MU-MIMO

The CSI-RS measurement computes CQI based on `interferingPackets` (line 176) вЂ” but during the CSI-RS measurement period, the interfering packets are from OTHER UEs' CSI-RS transmissions, NOT from the actual paired data transmissions. So CQI is fundamentally optimistic for MU-MIMO.

SINR in CSI = 31.9 dB (looks at own CSI-RS channel, interference в‰€ thermal noise only)


SINR in MU-MIMO data = much lower (interference from paired UEs not orthogonalized perfectly)

With SemiOrthogonalityFactor = 0.5, users that are only half-orthogonal can be paired, leaving significant residual interference that the CQI never accounts for.

## Root Cause 2: OLLA resets every CSI period (every 10 slots)

The OLLA offset resets frequently with a CSI period of 10 slots and roughly 7 DL slots per period, giving each UE only about 7 grants to accumulate feedback before the offset resets. With a StepUp of 0.27, it takes 4 NACKs just to add 1.08 to the offset, which isn't enough to adapt to the MU-MIMO interference before the cycle restarts.

The real issue is that OLLA can only reduce MCS by 2-3 steps per window at best, but the CQI is overestimating by 5-8 steps due to the unaccounted interference. I can fix this by setting a negative InitialOffset to start conservatively, removing the reset on CSI updates, or increasing the StepUp value significantly.

Looking more carefully at the OLLA mechanics: each CSI period resets the offset back to zero, so OLLA starts from scratch trying to compensate for the same overestimation problem repeatedly. Even though OLLA has reduced the average MCS from the reported ~27 down to 23-24, it's still not aggressive enough to hit the 10% BLER target.

The fundamental issue is that CQI 15 maps to MCS 27 based on single-user SINR assumptions, but MU-MIMO pairing creates significantly worse actual channel conditions. OLLA can only trim about 2 MCS steps per CSI period before resetting, which leaves a substantial gap between where the scheduler places the MCS and where it needs to be for acceptable performance.

I'm considering three potential fixes: pre-compensating with a higher InitialOffset to account for MU-MIMO's CQI overestimation, disabling the CSI reset so OLLA can accumulate corrections across multiple periods, or adjusting the step-up and step-down ratios for faster convergence. The current design treats each CSI period as independent with fresh CQI measurements, which works fine when CQI is accurate, but in MU-MIMO scenarios the CQI is systematically too optimistic, so resetting always forces the algorithm to start from an inflated baseline.

Looking at the actual performance gap, OLLA is managing to pull the MCS down by about 4 steps on average (from the CQI-predicted 27 to an actual 23), yet BLER is still hitting 50%, which tells me the true CQI overestimation is significantly larger than what OLLA is currently correcting for. The receiver's actual SINR is much lower than the reported 31.9 dB because of interference from the other MU-MIMO streams, and with up to 12 users each transmitting 4 layers, that's 48 concurrent streams competing for the same resources.

The semi-orthogonality factor of 0.5 is allowing user pairing with only 50% orthogonality, which compounds the interference problem. I'd prioritize increasing the InitialOffset to start each OLLA window more conservatively, then consider either removing the OLLA reset entirely or tightening the semi-orthogonality threshold to pair only more orthogonal users. Reducing the maximum paired users would also help, though that impacts throughput. The fact that some transmissions are at MCS 0 while the average sits around 23 suggests the algorithm is oscillating between extremes rather than settling into a stable operating point.

NhГ¬n vГ o output vГ  code, cГі **2 nguyГЄn nhГўn rГµ rГ ng** gГўy BLER cao:

---

## NguyГЄn nhГўn 1: CQI overestimation вЂ” structural MU-MIMO mismatch

CSI-RS Д‘o kГЄnh **single-user** (khГґng cГі interference tб»« paired UEs):
```
CQI = 15, SINR = 31.9 dB  в†ђ Д‘o lГєc chб»‰ cГі thermal noise
Actual SINR khi MU-MIMO  в†ђ thбєҐp hЖЎn nhiб»Ѓu vГ¬ paired UEs tбєЎo inter-user interference
```

Vб»›i `SemiOrthogonalityFactor = 0.5` cho phГ©p pairing cГЎc UE chб»‰ half-orthogonal в†’ residual interference cao. Scheduler dГ№ng MCS 24-27 (tб»« CQI=15) nhЖ°ng kГЄnh thб»±c tбєї khГґng chб»‹u Д‘Ж°б»Јc.

---

## NguyГЄn nhГўn 2: OLLA reset mб»—i CSI period вЂ” khГґng Д‘б»§ thб»ќi gian hб»™i tб»Ґ

TбєЎi [nrComponentCarrierContext.m:261](5g/netmod/+nr5g/+internal/nrComponentCarrierContext.m#L261):
```matlab
obj.MCSOffset(1) = schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigDL.InitialOffset;  % = 0
```

Mб»—i 10 slots в†’ reset vб»Ѓ 0 в†’ OLLA bбєЇt Д‘бє§u lбєЎi tб»« MCS cao (CQI-based). Trong 10 slots chб»‰ cГі ~5-7 DL grant/UE, vб»›i `StepUp = 0.27`:

```
Max offset sau 7 NACKs = 7 Г— 0.27 = 1.89  в†’  giбєЈm Д‘Ж°б»Јc tб»‘i Д‘a 1-2 MCS
NhЖ°ng gap thб»±c tбєї CQI vs MU-MIMO = 4-8 MCS  в†’  OLLA khГґng bГ№ Д‘б»§
```

Mб»—i CSI period mб»›i lбєЎi reset в†’ chu kб»і lбє·p lбєЎi mГЈi.

---

## Fix

**CГЎch 1 вЂ” nhanh nhбєҐt:** TДѓng `InitialOffset` Д‘б»ѓ pre-compensate MU-MIMO gap:

```matlab
% NRCell_128T128R_MUMIMO.m
```

content.thinking:
Now I have the full picture. The git history shows:

**Commit bc1f861 "fix: OLLA reset state"** is actually a **REVERT** of a previous fix!

The previous state (before bc1f861) had a comment:
```
% ThangTQ23 fix: do NOT reset MCSOffset here.
% OLLA accumulates MCSOffset via HARQ ACK/NACK (StepUp/StepDown)
% across the entire simulation. Resetting on every CSI report


% (every 5 ms) wiped the accumulated offset and caused BLER ~60%.
% Initialisation is already handled in the constructor.
```

So the PREVIOUS fix was to NOT reset, which gave better BLER. But commit bc1f861 **reverted** this fix back to the original reset behavior, with a new comment "by design per getMCSIndexOffset step 3".

The commit message says: "Without this, LinkAdaptationConfigDL=[] в†’ OLLA disabled в†’ BLER ~60%"

Looking at the diff more carefully, the old code (removed in this commit) had no resetвЂ”that was the ThangTQ23 fixвЂ”while the new code adds back the reset with Init, which is the opposite direction.

The commit actually made two changes: it reverted nrComponentCarrierContext.m from "no reset" back to "reset on CSI", and it added oLLAConfig and LinkAdaptationConfigDL to NRCell_128T128R_MUMIMO.m. So the previous state had no OLLA reset code and no LinkAdaptationConfigDL in configureScheduler (meaning OLLA was disabled), but bc1f861 added back the reset code with InitialOffset from config and enabled LinkAdaptationConfigDL in the scheduler.

The user's confusion makes sense nowвЂ”the commit message says the reset is "by design," but the actual BLER is still 35-59% even with OLLA enabled. The real problem I identified earlier remains: CQI=15 with SINR=31.9 dB in single-user mode, but MU-MIMO pairing causes severe degradation.

Looking at the actual performance, OLLA is reducing MCS from the initial 27 down to 23-24 on average, with occasional drops to 0, yet BLER stays stubbornly high. The issue is that OLLA resets every CSI period with InitialOffset=0, so it's constantly trying to converge within a 10-slot window before getting reset againвЂ”the gap between what CQI predicts and what the channel actually needs is just too large for OLLA to close in that timeframe.

The real problem is the MU-MIMO interference: with 12 users paired at 0.5 semi-orthogonality, each user's actual received SINR is much lower than the reported 31.9 dB, so even with OLLA's adjustments, the link stays unreliable. If OLLA weren't reset and could accumulate offset over time, it would eventually find a steady-state MCS where BLER matches the target, but the constant resets prevent that convergence.

Looking at the git history, the original code had no OLLA at all and suffered ~60% BLER, then someone added the "no reset" fix to let OLLA stabilize, but that was later reverted because disabling the OLLA config entirely also caused ~60% BLER вЂ” so the revert was actually re-enabling OLLA with resets rather than removing it completely.

The current ~50% BLER with resets is an improvement over no OLLA, but the user's real issue is the CQI-throughput mismatch: CQI is maxing out at 15 while throughput stays low, which points to a fundamental SINR estimation problem between single-user and multi-user scenarios rather than just an OLLA tuning issue. Looking at the actual commit diff, I can see the previous fix explicitly warned against resetting MCSOffset because it was wiping out the accumulated OLLA adjustments every 5ms and causing BLER to spike to 60%, but bc1f861 reintroduced the reset anyway with a "by design" comment that got cut off. The core issue is that systematic CQI overestimation in MU-MIMO means OLLA can't keep up with the reset cycle, so the real solution would be adjusting InitialOffset, removing the reset entirely, or tuning the SemiOrthogonalityFactor. The reset behavior appears intentional based on the standard specification, but for MU-MIMO scenarios where CQI estimates are systematically overoptimistic, resetting the OLLA per CSI period actually prevents convergence rather than helping it. So while the implementation matches the spec for single-user cases, it's still problematic for multi-user MIMO where the fundamental issue is biased channel feedback, not stale measurements.

Now I can clearly see the full picture. Let me explain this to the user:

1. The "ThangTQ23 fix" (previous version): do NOT reset в†’ OLLA accumulates в†’ BLER reduced from ~60%
2. bc1f861 REVERTED this fix and called it "by design" 
3. bc1f861 also ADDED oLLAConfig (OLLA was completely disabled before)
4. Result: OLLA now enabled but with reset в†’ partial improvement (60% в†’ ~50%)

The comment "Without this, LinkAdaptationConfigDL=[] в†’ OLLA disabled в†’ BLER ~60%" is referring to the fact that before the commit, `LinkAdaptationConfigDL` was not configured at all в†’ OLLA disabled completely. 

The "by design" reasoning comes from the standard getMCSIndexOffset algorithm which resets MCSOffset on each CSI reporting period, but this assumes accurate CQI feedbackвЂ”something that breaks down in MU-MIMO where CQI tends to be systematically overoptimistic. So reverting the no-reset fix actually undid the performance gains, leaving BLER stuck at ~50% instead of the ~40% improvement the accumulation strategy achieved.

But I need to reconsider what actually happened before bc1f861. The comment about LinkAdaptationConfigDL being empty and disabling OLLA entirely suggests the state before that commit had no OLLA configuration at all, which would explain the ~60% BLER. The "ThangTQ23 fix" removed the reset, but if OLLA wasn't even enabled due to missing config, that wouldn't have helped. Let me trace through what the actual configuration was in nrComponentCarrierContext.m after the "ThangTQ23 fix"...

Actually, I'm realizing the OLLA mechanism itself (the MCSOffset updates via StepUp/StepDown) happens in getMCSIndexOffset during HARQ feedback processing, not in updateChannelQualityDL. The reset in updateChannelQualityDL was just interfering with that accumulation. So removing the reset allows OLLA to work properly by letting the offset accumulate across CSI updates, which is why BLER improved. So the real issue is that OLLA wasn't even running before bc1f861 because LinkAdaptationConfigDL was missing entirelyвЂ”the "ThangTQ23 fix" just removed a reset that wasn't actually doing anything at the time. After bc1f861, OLLA got enabled and BLER dropped from ~60% to ~50%, but the reset is still limiting convergence. The current ~50% BLER stems from CQI=15 overestimating MCS for MU-MIMO, with OLLA partially compensating but held back by the resetвЂ”without it, the algorithm would adapt more effectively.

%% 3. Scheduler Configuration
% CSI measurement source: "CSI-RS" (eTypeII-r19 feedback) or "SRS" (TDD reciprocity)

csiMeasurementSignalDLType = "CSI-RS";
allocationType             = 0;   % RAT-0 (RBG-based)

% CSI-RS mode fields: SemiOrthogonalityFactor, MinCQI (not MinSINR вЂ” that is SRS-only)
muMIMOConfiguration = struct( ...
    MaxNumUsersPaired       = 4, ...
    MaxNumLayers            = 16, ...
    MinNumRBs               = 2, ...
    SemiOrthogonalityFactor = 0.7, ...
    MinCQI                  = 1);

% OLLA: TargetBLER = StepDown/(StepDown+StepUp) = 0.03/0.30 = 10%
% Resets to InitialOffset=0 on each CSI report (by design, fresh CQI window).
% Without this, LinkAdaptationConfigDL=[] в†’ OLLA disabled в†’ BLER ~60%.
oLLAConfig = struct('InitialOffset', 0, 'StepUp', 0.27, 'StepDown', 0.03);

configureScheduler(gNB, ...
    ResourceAllocationType  = allocationType, ...
    MaxNumUsersPerTTI       = 32, ...
    MUMIMOConfigDL          = muMIMOConfiguration, ...
    CSIMeasurementSignalDL  = csiMeasurementSignalDLType, ...
    LinkAdaptationConfigDL  = oLLAConfig);

%% 4. UE Deployment
% UEs scattered across a 120В° sector, min 50 m вЂ“ max 500 m from gNB.
% Distance drawn from uniform distribution on area (pdf в€ќ r) via sqrt-trick
% so that UEs are spread evenly over the sector area, not clustered near gNB.

content.thinking:
The user is asking if 20 frames is enough for testing State 3 (OLLA without reset, accumulating).

Let me think about this:

- OLLA needs time to accumulate MCSOffset
- CSI period = 10 slots
- 20 frames = 200 slots = 20 CSI periods per UE
- Each CSI period: ~5-7 DL slots (TDD), each UE gets some grants
- With 16 UEs and MaxNumUsersPaired=4, fewer UEs scheduled simultaneously
- Grants per UE per frame: rough estimate...

In 20 frames = 200 slots. TDD pattern: assuming 7DL/3UL (but we need to check actual TDD config). 

With 30 kHz SCS and 20 slots per frame, I'm calculating how many HARQ feedbacks each UE accumulates across 20 frames. That's roughly 400 total slots, with about 70% being DL slots in TDD mode, so around 280 DL slots available. Given the scheduling constraints with 16 UEs and MaxNumUsersPaired=4, each UE probably gets around 6-7 grants per frame, which should give OLLA enough feedback samples to start adjusting the MCS offset.

Now I'm working through the OLLA dynamics with StepUp=0.27. If a UE experiences roughly 50% NACK rate across 50-100 grants, that's 25-50 NACKs accumulating an offset of about 6.75 to 13.5 points, which translates to a 5-10 MCS reduction depending on where it lands in the adjustment curve.

The real question is whether 20 frames gives OLLA enough time to stabilize. Since OLLA converges when BLER matches the target (10%), and we're starting from 50%, the offset ramps up quickly initially. But the grants are distributed across many slots, so there's a lag between when NACKs occur and when the MCS adjustment takes effect. This means the first 10 frames likely show elevated BLER while the algorithm is still ramping, and only the latter frames approach steady-state behavior.

For a quick validation, 20 frames works if we're just checking the trendвЂ”whether MCS is actually decreasing and BLER is improving over time. But for a reliable BLER measurement, we'd need 50-100 frames to let the system fully settle. The grant summary gives us averages, though the scheduling logger does track per-frame data if we need to dig deeper. I should recommend looking at the MCS average (expecting it to drop from 23-24 down to maybe 15-20) and checking whether BLER shows improvement in the later frames compared to the earlier ones.
