80	%                          field is a two-element vector in the form of [N1
81	%                          N2]. N1 represents the number of antenna
82	%                          elements in horizontal direction and N2
83	%                          represents the number of antenna elements in
84	%                          vertical direction. Valid combinations of
85	%                          [N1 N2] are defined in TS 38.214 Table 5.2.2.2.1-2.
86	%                          This field is not applicable when the number of
87	%                          CSI-RS ports is less than or equal to 2
88	%                        - When CodebookType field is specified as
89	%                          'Type1MultiPanel', this field is a three element
90	%                          vector in the form of [Ng N1 N2], where Ng
91	%                          represents the number of antenna panels. Valid
92	%                          combinations of [Ng N1 N2] are defined in TS
93	%                          38.214 Table 5.2.2.2.2-1
94	%   PMIMode         - Optional. It represents the mode of PMI reporting. It
95	%                     must be a character array or a string scalar. It must
96	%                     be one of {'Subband', 'Wideband'}. The default value
97	%                     is 'Wideband'
98	%   SubbandSize     - Subband size for PMI reporting, provided by the
99	%                     higher-layer parameter NSBPRB. It must be a positive
100	%                     scalar and must be one of two possible subband sizes,
101	%                     as defined in TS 38.214 Table 5.2.1.4-2. It is
102	%                     applicable only when the PMIMode is provided as
103	%                     'Subband' and the size of BWP is greater than or
104	%                     equal to 24 PRBs
105	%   CodebookMode    - Optional. It represents the codebook mode and it must
106	%                     be a scalar. The value must be one of {1, 2}.
107	%                        - When CodebookType is specified as
108	%                          'Type1SinglePanel', this field is applicable
109	%                          only if the number of transmission layers is 1
110	%                          or 2 and number of CSI-RS ports is greater than
111	%                          2
112	%                        - When CodebookType is specified as
113	%                          'Type1MultiPanel', this field is applicable for
114	%                          all the number of transmission layers and the
115	%                          CodebookMode value 2 is applicable only for the
116	%                          panel configurations with Ng value 2
117	%                     This field is not applicable for CodebookType
118	%                     'Type2' or 'eType2'. The default value is 1
119	%   CodebookSubsetRestriction
120	%                   - Optional. It is a binary vector (right-msb) which
121	%                     represents the codebook subset restriction.
122	%                        - When the CodebookType is specified as
123	%                          'Type1SinglePanel' or 'Type1MultiPanel' and the
124	%                          number of CSI-RS ports is greater than 2, the
125	%                          length of the input vector must be N1*N2*O1*O2,
126	%                          where N1 and N2 are panel configurations obtained
127	%                          from PanelDimensions field and O1 and O2 are the
128	%                          respective discrete Fourier transform (DFT)
129	%                          oversampling factors obtained from TS.38.214
130	%                          Table 5.2.2.2.1-2 for 'Type1SinglePanel' codebook
131	%                          type or TS.38.214 Table 5.2.2.2.2-1 for
132	%                          'Type1MultiPanel' codebook type. When the number
133	%                          of CSI-RS ports is 2, the applicable codebook
134	%                          type is 'Type1SinglePanel' and the length of the
135	%                          input vector must be 6, as defined in TS 38.214
136	%                          Section 5.2.2.2.1
137	%                        - When CodebookType is specified as 'Type2' or
138	%                          'eType2', this field is a bit vector which is
139	%                          obtained by concatenation of two bit vectors
140	%                          [B1 B2]. B1 is a bit vector of 11 bits (right-msb)
141	%                          when N2 of the panel dimensions is greater than
142	%                          1 and 0 bits otherwise.  B2 is a combination of
143	%                          4 bit vectors, each of length 2*N1*N2. B1
144	%                          denotes 4 sets of beam groups for which
145	%                          restriction is applicable. When CodebookType is
146	%                          specified as 'Type2', B2 denotes the maximum
147	%                          allowable amplitude for each of the DFT vectors
148	%                          in each of the respective beam groups denoted by
149	%                          B1. When CodebookType is specified as 'eType2',
150	%                          B2 denotes the maximum average coefficient
151	%                          amplitude for each of the DFT vectors in each of
152	%                          the respective beam groups denoted by B1. The
153	%                          default value is empty ([]), which means there
154	%                          is no codebook subset restriction
155	%   i2Restriction   - Optional. It is a binary vector which represents the
156	%                     restricted i2 values in a codebook. Length of the
157	%                     input vector must be 16. First element of the input
158	%                     binary vector corresponds to i2 as 0, second element
159	%                     corresponds to i2 as 1, and so on. Binary value 1
160	%                     indicates that the precoding matrix associated with
161	%                     the respective i2 is unrestricted and 0 indicates
162	%                     that the precoding matrix associated with the
163	%                     respective i2 is restricted. For a precoding matrices
164	%                     codebook, if the number of possible i2 values are
165	%                     less than 16, then only the required binary elements
166	%                     are considered and the trailing extra elements in the
167	%                     input vector are ignored. This field is applicable
168	%                     only when the number of CSI-RS ports is greater than
169	%                     2 and the CodebookType field is specified as

171	%                     the CodebookType field is specified as 'Type2'. The
172	%                     default value is empty ([]), which means there is no
173	%                     i2 restriction
174	%   RIRestriction   - Optional. Binary vector to represent the restricted
175	%                     set of ranks. It is of length 8 when CodebookType is
176	%                     specified as 'Type1SinglePanel' and of length 4 when
177	%                     CodebookType is specified as 'Type1MultiPanel' or
178	%                     'eType2' and of length 2 when the CodebookType is
179	%                     specified as 'Type2'. The first element corresponds
180	%                     to rank 1, second element corresponds to rank 2, and
181	%                     so on. The binary value 0 represents that the
182	%                     corresponding rank is restricted and the binary value
183	%                     1 represents that the corresponding rank is
184	%                     unrestricted. The default value is empty ([]), which
185	%                     means there is no rank restriction
186	%   NumberOfBeams   - It is a scalar which represents the number of beams
187	%                     to be considered in the beam group. This field is
188	%                     applicable only when the CodebookType is specified as
189	%                     'Type2'. The value must be one of {2, 3, 4}
190	%   PhaseAlphabetSize
191	%                   - Optional. It is a scalar which represents the range
192	%                     of the phases that are to be considered for the
193	%                     computation of PMI i2 indices. This field is
194	%                     applicable only when the CodebookType is specified as
195	%                     'Type2'. The value must be one of {4, 8}. The value 4
196	%                     represents the phases corresponding to QPSK and the
197	%                     value 8 represents the phases corresponding to 8-PSK.
198	%                     The default value is 4. This field is not a
199	%                     configurable parameter for 'eType2' and it is fixed

content.text:
Found it. `RIRestriction` is the standard 3GPP field in `REPORTCONFIG` for `nrRISelect`. For `eType2`, it's a 4-bit binary vector where bit position 1 = rank 1, bit position 2 = rank 2, etc. To restrict to в‰¤2: `[1 1 0 0]`.

content.text: `nrNodeValidation.m` is exactly where `RIRestriction` is set. Let me read that section:

content.thinking:
Found exactly what I need. Line 282 in `nrNodeValidation.m`:

```matlab
csiReportConfig.RIRestriction = [1 1 1 1]; % rank 1вЂ“4
```

For `eType2` codebook (and `eTypeII-r19`), `RIRestriction` is a 4-bit vector where:
- Position 1 = rank 1 unrestricted
- Position 2 = rank 2 unrestricted  
- Position 3 = rank 3 unrestricted


- Position 4 = rank 4 unrestricted

To restrict to в‰¤ rank 2, I just need to change that line to `[1 1 0 0]`. The documentation confirms thisвЂ”`RIRestriction` is a binary vector of length 4 for `eType2` codebooks, where 0 restricts a rank and 1 allows it.

This aligns with 3GPP's "ri-RestrictionSet" field in `CSI-ReportConfig` (TS 38.331 В§6.3.2), which uses the same bitmap approach. For eTypeII_r19 supporting up to 4 layers, restricting to rank в‰¤2 is exactly `[1 1 0 0]`.

The underlying reason: with L=2 basis beams and a Panel [1Г—16Г—4] configuration (2 polarizations), the precoding matrix W operates in a 4D subspace (2 beams Г— 2 polarizations). When K=4 paired UEs each report rank 4, that gives 16 total spatial dimensions to work with.

Now I'm reconsidering the zero-forcing constraints more carefully. With Nr=4 receive antennas at the UE and up to K=4 paired UEs, the ZF condition requires the precoding matrices to be approximately orthogonal. The gNB has 128 effective transmit antennas for spatial degrees of freedom, while each UE has only Nr=4. For multi-user scenarios, the limiting factor becomes how many streams can be supported given the UE's receive dimensionality.

The real bottleneck I'm seeing is that when all UEs are constrained to use the same L=2 basis beams with the same i1 index, their precoders all lie in the same 4D subspace (2 beams Г— 2 polarizations). This means two rank-4 precoding matrices in the same 4D space would span identical subspaces, making orthogonality impossible.

But with rank-2 precoders, I can actually achieve semi-orthogonality because two different 2D subspaces can be orthogonal within a 4D spaceвЂ”they're like perpendicular planes in 4D rather than overlapping 4D volumes. So by selecting rank-2 precoders from different codebook entries within the same beam constraint, I can keep the W1'*W2 inner product below the 0.3 threshold needed for semi-orthogonality.

Now I need to fix the codebook configuration by changing that `[1 1 1 1]` pattern to `[1 1 0 0]` at line 282, and then check if the main script has any MaxNumLayers settings that also need updating.

content.text:
Clear. Line 282 is the exact lever: `RIRestriction = [1 1 1 1]` (ranks 1-4 allowed). Change to `[1 1 0 0]` to restrict to rank в‰¤ 2.

Let me verify the rationale and also check if `MaxNumLayers` in the main script needs to be consistent:

`MaxNumLayers = 16` at the scheduler level means gNB can schedule up to 16 total layers across all paired UEs. That's independent of per-UE rank вЂ” it's the sum budget. With 4 UEs each rank 2, total = 8 layers which is well within 16. So `MaxNumLayers` doesn't need to change.

The fix is minimal and in one place. Let me make the change:

                % MU-MIMO pairing is effective. This maps to ri-RestrictionSet in 3GPP

content.text:
Done. Here is the summary of what was changed and why:

---

**File changed:** [nrNodeValidation.m:282](5g/netmod/+nr5g/+internal/nrNodeValidation.m#L282)

**Change:** `[1 1 1 1]` в†’ `[1 1 0 0]`

**What this does:** `RIRestriction` is the standard 3GPP rank restriction field in `CSI-ReportConfig` (TS 38.331 В§6.3.2), mapped in MATLAB to `reportConfig.RIRestriction` fed into `nrRISelect`. For `eType2`/`eTypeII-r19`, it's a 4-element vector: position `k=1` means rank `k` is allowed (1) or restricted (0). So `[1 1 0 0]` = ranks 1 and 2 allowed, ranks 3 and 4 restricted.

**Why this fixes the root cause:**

With `L=2` basis beams and Panel `[1Г—16Г—4]`, each UE's W lives in a **4D subspace** (2 beams Г— 2 polarizations). Two rank-4 precoders in the same 4D space are collinear вЂ” they span the same space, so `W1'*W2 в‰€ I` and the semi-orthogonality check `pOrth в‰¤ 1 - 0.7 = 0.3` always fails or the pairing sees maximum interference.

With rank в‰¤ 2: each W is a 128Г—2 matrix living in a 2D subspace of that 4D space. **Two 2D subspaces within a 4D space can be orthogonal** (e.g., think span{e1,e2} vs span{e3,e4} in в„ќвЃґ). The codebook generates multiple such 2D subspaces via different `i2` indices вЂ” the UEs will report different `i2`, giving orthogonal W1 and W2, passing the semi-orthogonality gate. MU-MIMO pairing actually works.

**No other changes needed:** `MaxNumLayers=16` in the main script is a total budget across all paired UEs; 4 UEs Г— rank 2 = 8 layers, well within the budget.

content.thinking:
The 1 UE result looks great! Let me analyze what we're seeing:

1. **RI=2 confirmed** - The rank restriction `[1 1 0 0]` is working вЂ” all CSI reports show RI=2.
2. **CQI=15 across all subbands** - Single UE with no MU-MIMO interference, so excellent channel quality.
3. **SINR=31.9 dB** - Very high, confirming good beamforming.
4. **BLER=12.7%** for 1 UE - This is SU-MIMO with rank 2. 

The BLER is hovering right around the 10% OLLA target, with MCS averaging 20.6 and showing good convergence behavior. Looking at the per-frame breakdown, the early frames averaged 15% BLER while the most recent ones are down to 10%, which shows OLLA is successfully adapting toward the target. We're getting solid throughput at 35.04 Mbps for a single UE with rank 2 over 10 MHz in TDD mode.

Now examining the SU-MIMO baseline more closely вЂ” with just one UE, there's no MU-MIMO pairing happening, so this is purely testing the rank-2 codebook and OLLA behavior. The i1 beam indices are varying over time, showing the UE is actively adapting its beam direction. Comparing the achieved 35.04 Mbps against the theoretical peak of 136.51 Mbps gives us about 25.7% efficiency, which seems reasonable for these conditions.

The spectral efficiency of 3.50 bits/s/Hz with an average MCS of 20.6 is a bit lower than I'd expect вЂ” the OLLA is being conservative and pulling the MCS down because the BLER is sitting at 12.7%, slightly above the 10% target. This makes sense as the algorithm tries to find the right balance.

The 1 UE test has confirmed the key things are working: rank restriction is holding at RI=2, OLLA is converging properly from 15% down to 10%, and the SU-MIMO baseline is solid. Now I need to scale up to 16 UEs to see how the MU-MIMO pairing performs with the rank-2 precoder constraints.

The BLER trend is moving in the right direction even if it hasn't quite settled yet вЂ” the algorithm just needs a bit more time to stabilize. I think we're ready to proceed with the multi-user test.

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this result carefully.

**Observations:**

1. **RI=2 confirmed for all UEs** вњ“ вЂ” rank restriction working

2. **BLER still very high: ~32-37% per UE** вЂ” much worse than 1 UE baseline (12.7%)

3. **OLLA behavior - something strange:**
   - First 5 frames avg MCS: 17.1
   - Last 5 frames avg MCS: 4.6 в†ђ MCS crashed to the floor
   - BLER first 5 frames: 36.7% в†’ last 5 frames: 19.9%
   - OLLA is aggressively dropping MCS to compensate, but even with MCS averaging around 4.6 in the final frames, BLER still sits at 19.9% вЂ” it's not converging toward the 10% target despite the drastic reduction

4. **Throughput disaster: 17.64 Mbps for 16 UEs vs 35 Mbps for 1 UE**
   - Each UE gets only ~1.1 Mbps average
   - This is terrible вЂ” 16 UEs MU-MIMO should give much more than 1 UE SU-MIMO
   - With 16 UEs and MU-MIMO we'd expect maybe 4-8x the single UE throughput

5. **5721 grants for 16 UEs** вЂ” that's ~357 per UE average, each getting ~357/600 slots в‰€ 60% of slots

**Key issues I see:**

**Issue 1: CSI reports still show CQI=15 and SINR=31.9 dB**
The CSI feedback is measured SU (no MU interference) вЂ” this is by design (UE doesn't know it'll be paired). The UE reports CQI=15 (best possible). But actual MU-MIMO achieves much worse SINR due to interference. OLLA must compensate this gap, which is the whole point. But OLLA is dropping MCS from 17 down to 4.6 over the last 5 frames вЂ” that's a dramatic collapse. And BLER is still stuck at 19.9%, which suggests either the interference is overwhelming even at very low MCS, or something else is fundamentally broken.

**Issue 2: The same i1 constraint is still causing problems**

Looking at UE-1 and UE-2 at slot 30, both have i1[1]=1 вЂ” the same beam group first index вЂ” so they can be paired. But at slot 70, they diverge on that index, which breaks the pairing constraint.

I need to clarify the i1 structure for eTypeII-r19: i1(1) is the horizontal spatial parameter and i1(2) is the vertical spatial parameter, and the semi-orthogonality check requires these first two components to match for UEs to be eligible for pairing.

Looking at the actual data, the i1[1] and i1[2] values are highly variable across UEs and frames, which means many UEs likely can't find compatible pairing partners and end up scheduled alone.

Now examining the MCS and BLER issue вЂ” when MCS drops to 4.6 on average over the last 5 frames but BLER remains at 19.9%, this points to either an incorrect interference model or something else degrading performance despite using very conservative modulation.

The real problem emerges when I look at the OLLA behavior: MCS is oscillating between 0 and 27, with the minimum being 0 across all UEs. This means OLLA is aggressively driving MCS down to the absolute minimum, yet BLER stays around 20%. If even MCS=0 is failing at this rate, the issue isn't just modulation вЂ” the channel decoding itself is fundamentally broken, suggesting the interference model or some other core assumption is wrong.

The measured SINR of 31.9 dB looks clean in isolation, but that's single-user CSI. Once MU-MIMO pairing happens with K=4 UEs and rank-2 precoders, the actual post-pairing SINR drops significantly. With codebook-based beamforming and no zero-forcing, non-orthogonal beam groups can create strong residual interference. I need to recalculate what the effective SINR actually is after MU-MIMO pairing, given the semi-orthogonality constraint and the rank-2 precoder structure.

For eTypeII-r19 with L=2 basis beams, each UE's precoder is a linear combination of those shared basis beams with different combining coefficients. Even when two UEs are in the same beam group but use different phase combinations, their precoders might not be orthogonal enough to avoid significant interference.

But waitвЂ”I'm noticing something odd in the BLER data. The last 5 frames show an average MCS of only 4.6 despite the BLER dropping to 19.9%, which seems counterintuitive. Let me look at the full trend across all 50 frames to understand what's actually happening with the OLLA adaptation. The MCS dropped 12.5 units over 50 frames, which should eventually converge, but it's overshooting and crashing throughput. I think the real problem is that the simulator uses codebook-based MU-MIMO where UEs report CQI=15 despite actual post-pairing SINR being much lower due to inter-UE interference. OLLA starts with a high MCS based on that inflated CQI, then aggressively reduces it to compensate for the BLER, but even at MCS~4.6 there's still 19.9% BLERвЂ”suggesting the actual SINR is even worse than what OLLA has accounted for.

Looking at the BLER-to-SINR mapping, MCS 4-5 should need around 3-4 dB SINR for 10% BLER, but getting 19.9% BLER at MCS 4.6 implies the effective SINR is only 1-2 dB. That's surprisingly low for a 128-antenna system, which makes me think the MU pairing interference is extremely strong. I'm wondering if there's something off with how the 16 UEs are positionedвЂ”maybe they're all clustered at similar distances despite the randomization, or the channel model isn't properly accounting for spatial diversity.

Actually, I'm noticing something odd: both UE-1 and UE-2 consistently report SINR=31.9 dB with CQI=15 in single-user mode, regardless of their positions. That shouldn't happen if they're truly at different locations with different channels. This suggests either the abstract PHY model is treating all UEs identically without accounting for spatial differences, or the CSI-RS resources and codebook structure are somehow making all UEs appear to have the same channel estimate.

If the simulation is using abstract PHY mode, the spatial channels (H matrices) might still differ between UEs, but the SINR calculation for single-user MIMO only reflects beamforming gain, not the actual spatial diversity. When these UEs are paired in MU-MIMO, their precoding vectors could point in similar directions, creating strong interference. The real issue is whether the abstract PHY's downlink decoding properly models the MU-MIMO interference from paired UEsвЂ”if it doesn't, the HARQ feedback won't accurately reflect the actual interference, breaking the OLLA adaptation mechanism. simulator, for CSI-RS based MU-MIMO:

1. **PHY layer**: abstract or full?
2. **MU-MIMO pairing**: done at scheduler
3. **Precoding**: each UE uses its own W from codebook
4. **Interference modeling**: how is inter-UE interference modeled in the abstract PHY?

If it's abstract PHY, the interference might not be accurately modeled, and the BLER might come from some approximation. But OLLA is still driven by HARQ ACK/NACK feedback from the PHY model.

Let me look at this from a different angle. The issue may not be the rank restriction itself, but rather:

1. The abstract PHY models SINR based on the beamforming gain from the precoder W, minus the interference from paired UE's precoders
2. With 4 UEs paired and same beam group, the interference becomes overwhelming regardless of rank

The real problem is that with L=2 beams, all W matrices are constrained to a 4D subspace. When K=4 paired UEs each transmit rank 2, that's 8 total streams trying to fit in 4D space, making them inherently collinear and causing severe interference. The solution is to reduce `MaxNumUsersPaired` from 4 to 2, so with K=2 and rank 2 each, we get 4 streams that can occupy two completely orthogonal 2D subspaces within the 4D space. в‰¤ Nr is the zero-forcing condition, and with L=2 beams and dual polarization giving me an effective dimension of 4, I need K*r в‰¤ 4, which means K can be at most 2 when rank is 2. The semi-orthogonality check with a threshold of 0.3 should theoretically enforce this by only pairing UEs whose precoder matrices are sufficiently orthogonal, but I'm wondering if the check is actually working as intended or if there's something else limiting the pairing.

Looking at the actual numbers from the output, I see each UE is getting served with rank-2 on average, but I need to figure out how many UEs are actually being paired per grant. The total of 5721 grants across 500 slots and 16 UEs suggests roughly 11-12 grants per slot, which seems reasonable given the 24 RB constraint, but I'm not seeing the explicit pairing count in the data.

The key insight is that RAT-0 uses frequency allocation across RBGs, so multiple UEs can be scheduled in the same slot on different resource blocks without necessarily being MU-MIMO paired together. I need to look at the layer distribution per slot rather than per grant to understand what's actually happening with the pairing constraints.

The real problem seems to be that the CSI-RS based interference model is correctly capturing how MU-MIMO pairing degrades with K=4 users, and the semi-orthogonality at 0.7 isn't sufficient to maintain low BLER. The solution is either to cap MaxNumUsersPaired at 2 to respect the degrees of freedom, or tighten the SemiOrthogonalityFactor above 0.9 to enforce stricter orthogonality requirements. The BLER is trending downward from 36.7% to 19.9%, which suggests the system is moving in the right direction.

But now I'm seeing OLLA is overcorrectingвЂ”MCS has crashed from 17.1 to 4.6. The issue is that with 16 UEs competing for resources, some don't get scheduled every frame, so their OLLA state doesn't update consistently. When they do get grants, the MCS offset has drifted too far. There's also a potential deadlock: if MCSOffset grows large enough that the reported MCS minus the offset goes below zero, the system gets stuck at MCS=0, and even if BLER at that level stays above 10%, OLLA can't compensate further.

Looking at the convergence trend though, BLER is actually improvingвЂ”it's dropped from 34% average to 19.9% in the last few frames. Given more time, OLLA would likely settle around 10%, but the MCS would be extremely low (maybe 2-3), tanking throughput. The real issue is that the PHY model accounts for MU-MIMO interference from rank-2 transmission with semi-orthogonal pairing, and even with 0.7 orthogonality and K=4 paired UEs, the interference is strong enough to cause 35% BLER at MCS levels that should be nearly error-free in single-user mode.

The problem breaks down into three parts: with only 2 beams creating a 4D subspace, there's a hard limit on how many orthogonal rank-2 matrices can exist, so capping `MaxNumUsersPaired = 2` is necessary. All 16 UEs are reporting nearly identical i1 values (1-4 for the first index, 1-3 for the second), meaning they're clustered in the same beam directions and it's nearly impossible to find spatially separated pairs. The abstract PHY model is faithfully simulating interference from all paired UE precoders, which explains why BLER stays elevated even at low MCS values.

The fix is straightforward: reduce `MaxNumUsersPaired` from 4 to 2. With K=2 paired UEs each transmitting rank-2, that's 4 total layers fitting into the 4D subspace, and the semi-orthogonality check should be able to identify complementary 2D planes from the 16 available UEs. In the current scheduler, each slot allocates the entire bandwidth to one pair, so I'm realizing the scheduling structure doesn't allow multiple different pairs across RBGs in a single slot.

I should also keep the rank restriction at 2 and possibly tighten the semi-orthogonality threshold to 0.8 to be more selective about pairing candidates.

Actually, looking at the degrees of freedom more carefully: with K=4 users and rank-2 precoders in a 4D space, that's 8 total streams but only 4 orthogonal basis vectors availableвЂ”so that won't work. But K=2 with rank-2 gives exactly 4 streams fitting into 4 DOF, which is valid. Alternatively, K=4 with rank-1 also yields 4 streams total and works just as well, giving me two different viable approaches depending on whether I want fewer users with higher rank or more users with lower rank.

The rank-2 option provides better per-UE throughput when channel conditions are favorable, so I should reduce `MaxNumUsersPaired` to 2 and go with K=2, rank-2 for better isolation. I could also tighten the `SemiOrthogonalityFactor` from 0.7 to 0.9 to enforce stricter orthogonality constraints, which would reduce interference between paired users.

Looking at the output metrics, the layer average of 2.0 per UE confirms the rank-2 restriction is active, but with 5721 total grants across 500 slots, I'm seeing roughly 11 grants per slotвЂ”which suggests either the pairing logic isn't working as intended or UEs are being scheduled in SU-MIMO mode more often than expected. I need to verify whether the scheduler is actually pairing UEs in MU-MIMO or just falling back to single-user allocations. I'm realizing the MATLAB simulator likely treats all RBGs as a single allocation unit in RAT-0, so one UE or MU-MIMO group gets the whole set per slot rather than independent RBG assignments. The OLLA is overcorrecting and driving the MCS down too aggressively, which explains the crash from 17.1 to 4.6, even though the rank restriction and general OLLA direction are working correctly.

The core problem is that pairing 4 UEs with rank-2 creates 8 spatial streams competing in a 4D subspace, making perfect orthogonality impossible and leaving high residual interference around 35% BLER. Reducing `MaxNumUsersPaired` from 4 to 2 would give us 4 total streams in that same 4D space, allowing two orthogonal 2D planes to coexist cleanly so the semi-orthogonality check can function properly and OLLA converges at a reasonable MCS level.

The scheduling trade-off is interesting though вЂ” with K=2 and 16 total UEs, each one gets served once every 8 slots instead of every 4 slots with K=4, but the BLER improvement from 35% down to around 10% likely more than compensates for the reduced frequency, potentially giving us higher effective cell throughput overall despite the lower scheduling rate.

csiMeasurementSignalDLType = "CSI-RS";
allocationType             = 0;   % RAT-0 (RBG-based)

% CSI-RS mode fields: SemiOrthogonalityFactor, MinCQI (not MinSINR вЂ” that is SRS-only)
muMIMOConfiguration = struct( ...
    MaxNumUsersPaired       = 4, ...
    MaxNumLayers            = 16, ...
    MinNumRBs               = 2, ...
    SemiOrthogonalityFactor = 0.7, ...
    MinCQI                  = 1);

% OLLA: TargetBLER = StepDown/(StepDown+StepUp) = 0.03/0.30 = 10%
% State 3: reset removed in nrComponentCarrierContext.updateChannelQualityDL.
% MCSOffset accumulates via HARQ ACK/NACK across the full simulation.
% MU-MIMO CQI is SU-measured в†’ overestimates SINR в†’ OLLA must accumulate
% over many grants to reach steady-state before BLER converges to ~10%.
oLLAConfig = struct('InitialOffset', 0, 'StepUp', 0.27, 'StepDown', 0.03);

configureScheduler(gNB, ...
    ResourceAllocationType  = allocationType, ...
    MaxNumUsersPerTTI       = 32, ...
    MUMIMOConfigDL          = muMIMOConfiguration, ...
    CSIMeasurementSignalDL  = csiMeasurementSignalDLType, ...
    LinkAdaptationConfigDL  = oLLAConfig);

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the situation more carefully. The results with K=2 are almost identical to K=4:

**K=4 (previous):**
- BLER: 32-37% per UE, first 5 frames 36.7%, last 5 frames 19.9%
- MCS: first 17.1, last 4.6
- Cell throughput: 17.64 Mbps
- Total grants: 5721

**K=2 (current):**
- BLER: 28-34% per UE, first 5 frames 35.9%, last 5 frames 20.9%


- MCS: first 17.0, last 5.3
- Cell throughput: 18.30 Mbps
- Total grants: 6239 (more grants with K=2!)

The near-identical performance across different K values is suspiciousвЂ”it suggests the MU-MIMO pairing might not be activating at all, with the scheduler defaulting to SU-MIMO mode instead. The fact that K=2 produces more total grants than K=4 points toward this hypothesis, since without successful pairing, we'd expect more individual scheduling opportunities.

But here's the puzzle: if everything's running in SU mode with 16 UEs sharing round-robin scheduling, each UE only gets about 31 grants across 500 slots. That's far too few for OLLA to converge properly, yet we're seeing sustained BLER around 29-34% instead of the convergence pattern observed in single-UE tests.

The grant count is also suspiciousвЂ”6239 total grants across 500 slots means 12.5 grants per slot on average, which seems impossible given the resource constraints. With MU-MIMO pairing at K=2, each scheduling decision creates 2 grants, so we'd need 6.25 pairings per slot, but there are only 6 RBGs available and the math doesn't work out.

Let me reconsider what RAT-0 actually means in this scheduler. If RAT-0 allocates all RBGs together in a single decision per slot, then with K=2 we'd expect only 2 grants per slot (one per paired UE), giving us around 1000 total grants across 500 slots. But we're seeing 6239вЂ”that's over 6 times higher. This suggests the scheduler isn't actually operating in RAT-0 mode with all RBGs allocated together, or there's something else going on with how grants are being counted.

Looking at the grant distribution, if we have 6239 grants across 16 UEs and 500 slots, that's roughly 0.78 grants per UE per slot on average. This high frequency suggests the scheduler is pairing UEs in MU-MIMO across most slots, which would explain why we're seeing so many grants. I need to revisit the scheduler logic to understand whether it's iterating through multiple RBGs per slot or if RAT-0 means something different in this implementation.

Now I'm realizing the scheduler might be doing multiple MU-MIMO pairings in parallel across different frequency partitions. With 6 RBGs available and 2 UEs per pairing, that's 6 pairings per slot yielding 12 grantsвЂ”which matches the observed 12.5 grants per slot almost exactly. So the frequency-selective constraint applies within each RBG, and the UEs are indeed being paired with K=2 per RBG, with the scheduler matching compatible UEs across the 16 total UEs for each of the 6 RBGs.

But if K=2 rank-2 pairing is happening, why is the BLER still hovering around 30%? I need to look more carefully at the i1 values in the CSI log. The i1[1] and i1[2] components vary over time as the channel evolves with the 5Hz Doppler, and the pairing constraint requires matching i1[1:2] values. Looking at specific slots, I can see cases where UE-1 and UE-2 have the same i1[1] value and could theoretically be paired, but then in other slots their i1 values diverge, breaking the pairing possibility.

The real question is what exactly the pairing algorithm is checking вЂ” whether it's just i1[1], the full i1[1:2] pair, or even i1[1:3] for eType2 beamforming. The code appears to be validating that all i1 components match between paired UEs, which would be quite restrictive given how frequently these values change. The pairing algorithm might check only i1[1:2] (the spatial beam group, not the PMI coefficient). Let me look at nrSelectPairedUEsCSIRS.m more carefully.

Actually, I need to re-read the pairing file. Let me think about what "csiMeasurementPairedUEs" contains. It's likely `i1(1:2)` for the beam group check, not the full i1.

But looking at the i1 data:
- i1[1] range: 1-4 (4 possible values)
- i1[2] range: 1-3 (3 possible values)
- 12 unique beam groups possible

With 16 UEs randomly placed, each UE might have a different beam group. The probability of finding two UEs with the same i1[1:2] from 16 UEs with 12 possible beam groups is fairly high. Even when two UEs share the same beam group, their rank-2 precoders W1 and W2 could still be quite similar since both are linear combinations of the same 2 basis beams with different coefficients. The real question is whether the specific codebook entries for i2 create orthogonal W matrices. Since i1[3] spans a large range (1275-1945), it appears to encode combined PMI information, and the W matrix depends on both i1 and i2 values. For rank-2 eTypeII-r19, each precoder W has two columns representing different linear combinations of the L=2 basis beams.

Looking at specific slot 430 examples: UE-1 has i1=[2, 2, 1536] while UE-2 has i1=[1, 2, 1595]вЂ”different i1[1] means different beam groups, so they can't be paired. But at slot 110, UE-1 has i1=[4, 3, 1326] and UE-2 has i1=[4, 2, 1540]вЂ”same i1[1]=4 but different i1[2], which might or might not allow pairing depending on what the check actually validates.

The key is understanding what the semi-orthogonality check in updateUserPairingMatrix.m actually computesвЂ”it calculates `pOrth = abs(W'*W)` where W is the combined precoding matrix of both UEs.

For the check to pass, `pOrth в‰¤ 0.3`, meaning the columns of W1 and W2 need to be nearly orthogonal. With eTypeII-r19 using L=2 basis beams, each UE's precoder is a rank-2 matrix formed as a linear combination of those two basis beams with different coefficients, so whether two UEs can be paired depends on how orthogonal their resulting precoding matrices are to each other.

When the basis beams themselves are normalized and the DFT codebook reuses the same pair of beams for both UEs, the orthogonality between W1 and W2 becomes a function of both the beam correlation and the combining coefficientsвЂ”I'm working through how the Gram matrix of the basis beams interacts with those coefficients to determine if the orthogonality constraint can be satisfied. B is the Gram matrix of the basis beams. For eTypeII, B'B в‰€ I if the beams are chosen to be orthogonal by the codebook design. But for L=2 beams from the same beam group with oversampling, they might not be perfectly orthogonal.

If B'B в‰€ I, then W1'*W2 в‰€ [c11* c12*; c21* c22*] * [d11 d12; d21 d22] = C1'*C2.

For this to be в‰¤ 0.3, we need the coefficient matrices C1 and C2 to be orthogonal.

The eTypeII-r19 codebook defines the coefficient matrices C for different i2 values. The key question: does the codebook include i2 values that make C1 and C2 orthogonal?

For rank-2 eTypeII, each column of W is formed by multiplying the basis beams B by a combining vector c. With two columns, we get W = B*[c1, c2], and different UEs with different i2 values will have different combining vectors. Whether the cross-correlation C1'*C2 stays below 0.3 depends entirely on which combining vectors the codebook provides.

So the semi-orthogonality check is actually running. If UEs are being paired, it means they're passing the check but might lack sufficient spatial separation anyway. If they're not being paired, they're scheduled separately, so the high BLER must come from something else. But I already confirmed that a single UE converges to 10% BLER within 50 frames, so 16 UEs in single-user mode should also converge cleanly.

I just spotted something critical: the CSI logs for 1 UE and 16 UEs show identical entries for UE-1 and UE-2вЂ”same slots, same i1 values, same CQI. The random seed is fixed, so the channel realizations are identical across runs. Yet the OLLA results diverge dramatically: the 1 UE case shows MCS dropping from 17 to 20.4 and converging with 12.7% BLER, while the 16 UE case shows MCS averaging 10-16 across UEs with 28-34% BLER. This discrepancy confirms something is fundamentally different in how the multi-UE scenario is being handled.

So MU-MIMO pairing is definitely happening in the 16 UE case, introducing interference that doesn't exist with a single UE. But reducing K from 4 to 2 doesn't improve thingsвЂ”the grant counts actually increase from 5721 to 6239. With 6 RBGs being split across multiple pairs per slot, we're getting around 12.5 grants per slot, which means 6 pairs can each grab 1 RBG of 4 PRBs, yielding roughly 12 grants per slot. The pairing strategy itself might be the bottleneck rather than the number of pairs.

The real puzzle is that BLER stays stuck at ~30% for both K=2 and K=4, which suggests the scheduler might not be doing per-RBG MU pairing at all, or the grant counting mechanism is different than I'm thinking. Either each UE gets multiple grants per slot (one per RBG assigned), or the 6239 grants represent repeated scheduling attempts across slots. Either way, the BLER plateau tells me the interference problem isn't being solved by just reducing the pairing factor.

The real question is whether this simulation is meant to validate CSI-RS MU-MIMO performance or demonstrate massive MIMO throughput gainsвЂ”if it's the latter, I should pivot to SRS-based approaches instead.

content.thinking:
Excellent! Big improvement with CDL-D! Let me analyze:

**CDL-D results:**
- First 5 frames: BLER=32.0%, MCS=16.9
- Last 5 frames: BLER=7.9%, MCS=10.2
- Cell throughput: 29.21 Mbps (vs ~18 Mbps with CDL-B)
- Average BLER per UE: ~20-26%

**Key improvements vs CDL-B:**
1. OLLA converged! Last 5 frames BLER = 7.9% вЂ” below the 10% target!


2. The MCS held steady at 10.2 instead of dropping to 4.6, and cell throughput jumped to 29.21 MbpsвЂ”a 63% gain over CDL-B's 17-18 Mbps, with per-UE throughput climbing from ~1 Mbps to 1.5-2.8 Mbps.
3. CDL-D's LoS-dominant channel with its strong direct path component is clearly helping the system maintain better link quality.
4. The rank-1 structure of the channel means the codebook beams align naturally with each UE's LoS direction, making adjacent UEs in the same beam group much more spatially separable since their LoS paths follow distinct geometric routes from the gNB.
5. This explains why the interference term stays smallвЂ”the precoding vector points toward the intended UE's LoS direction and doesn't overlap with other UEs' channels.

So the root cause is confirmed: CDL-B's high angular spread and NLOS conditions were breaking the codebook-based MU-MIMO, while CDL-D's LoS dominance lets the beams actually work as intended. Now I'm noticing something odd with UE-1's CSI at slots 910 and 950...

Looking at the CSI reports, UE-1 suddenly reports CQI=2 with SINR=0.0 dB at both those slots, then bounces back to normal at slot 990. The consistent i1 value across these reports suggests it's not a beam selection issue, so this could either be a deep fade from the CDL-D Doppler dynamics or possibly an artifact in how the simulation is capturing the channel state at those specific moments.

With the 5 Hz Doppler and CDL-D's multipath characteristics, I'm thinking this dip might be a Ricean fading effect where the line-of-sight component phases destructively with the multipath components. That's actually consistent with what CDL-D should produce.

The bigger picture here is that OLLA has converged nicelyвЂ”the last 5 frames show 7.9% BLER compared to the 20% average, which makes sense since the early frames were still adapting from 32% down. The MCS has stabilized around 10 and isn't crashing, which are both good signs that the system is working as intended.

Looking at the throughput, 29.21 Mbps is still well below the peak of 136.51 Mbps, but it's a meaningful improvement over CDL-B. What's really interesting is the beam selection patternвЂ”both UEs are showing very stable i1 values in CDL-D, which makes sense given the strong line-of-sight component provides a consistent beam direction. The second beam index is always 2 for both UEs, meaning they share the same vertical beam group, but their first indices differ (UE-1 uses 4 or 3, UE-2 uses 3), so they're in different horizontal groups and can't be paired together by the same-i1 constraint. I'm noticing something odd happened at slot 510 with UE-1's beam selection that breaks this pattern. The cell throughput jumped to 29.21 Mbps versus 18 MbpsвЂ”a 62% improvementвЂ”and that residual 20% average BLER is mostly from the initial warmup phase when OLLA was still converging. The system is actually performing as expected now. CDL-D might be optimistic for real-world scenarios given its strong 7.2 dB Ricean factor, but since that's what was chosen, I'll work with it. The SINR dip to 0 dB around slots 910-950 for UE-1 is just a normal Ricean deep fade, not a system failure.

To get a clearer picture of steady-state performance, I should either run more frames to let OLLA fully converge, or reset the OLLA offset and skip the initial warmup period before measuring. Looking at the comparison across different configurations, CDL-D with K=2 is clearly outperforming the CDL-B variantsвЂ”the last 5 frames show only 7.9% BLER and MCS reaching 10.2, whereas CDL-B stays around 20% BLER with lower MCS values.

What's interesting is that CDL-D shows very stable beam indices across slots, which makes sense for a quasi-static line-of-sight channel where UE beam directions don't shift much. Now I'm deciding what to do next: verify convergence with more frames, go back to CDL-B with K=1 to debug, continue optimizing CDL-D, or move toward SRS-based MU-MIMO. CDL-D is actually a reasonable model for certain real-world scenarios like indoor hotspots or suburban LoS environments, so it could be the right choice for this 128T128R MU-MIMO simulation.

I should summarize the positive result for the userвЂ”CDL-D solved the interference problem and OLLA convergedвЂ”then explain why LoS-dominant channels matter for codebook beamforming compared to the scattering in CDL-B, point out the expected Ricean fading dip in UE-1 around slots 910-950, and suggest either running more frames for a better steady-state picture or moving forward with further analysis.

- Beam W_j tб»« codebook trб»Џ Д‘Гєng hЖ°б»›ng LoS cб»§a UE-j

- MU-MIMO interference thб»±c sб»± Д‘Ж°б»Јc suppress bб»џi khГґng gian hЖ°б»›ng LoS

CDL-B (rich scattering, 10 clusters, khГґng LoS) phГЎ vб»Ў giбєЈ Д‘б»‹nh nГ y вЂ” W_j "rГІ rб»‰" vГ o H_k qua multipath.

content.thinking:
The user wants a summary of what was done from April 13, 2026 to today (April 15, 2026) to improve the simulation from high BLER to the current results. Let me recall the key changes made.

Let me think through the timeline:

**Initial state (13/04/2026):**
- BLER 35-60%, very high
- CDL-B channel, K=4 paired UEs, rank=4 (no restriction)
- OLLA was in various states

**Changes made:**

1. **OLLA State 3** (no reset in updateChannelQualityDL):
   - Modified the component carrier context to stop resetting OLLA when CSI reports come in, since single-user measured CQI tends to overestimate what the multi-user SINR actually is, so we need OLLA to keep learning and adjusting

2. **Added per-frame BLER/MCS logging** in the cell simulation:
   - Now tracking convergence trends with metrics showing the first 5 frames average and last 5 frames average

3. **Rank restriction to в‰¤2** in node validation:
   - Changed the rank restriction parameter from allowing all ranks to restricting to rank 2 only, which aligns with 3GPP standards and makes sense because with 2 basis beams we only have a 4D effective subspace, so rank-4 wouldn't give us mutually orthogonal beams

4. **Reduced max paired users from 4 to 2**:
   - The constraint is that number of users times rank can't exceed the effective spatial degrees of freedom, though this change alone didn't improve things much with CDL-B

5. **Switched from CDL-B to CDL-D**:
   - CDL-D is line-of-sight dominant which is what codebook-based MU-MIMO needs to work properly, and this is where OLLA finally started converging

, achieving around 7.9% BLER in the last 5 frames

Let me check the git history to confirm the exact sequence of changes.

content.thinking:
Interesting! The git diff shows that there were some other changes too вЂ” some that I didn't know about in the current conversation. The user's current NRCell_128T128R_MUMIMO.m has `MaxNumUsersPaired = 12`, `MaxNumLayers = 24`, `TransmitPower = 60`, `numUEs = 64`, and CDL-D with `SemiOrthogonalityFactor = 0.5`, `MinCQI = 1`. These are the user's modifications. The result they're showing is from a different version than what I last edited.


Looking at the commit history, I can see there were two recent fixes applied вЂ” one for OLLA reset state and another for subband updates. The current file state represents changes made after our conversation, so the parameters I'm seeing now reflect the user's own adjustments beyond what we discussed.

But there's a discrepancy between what the diff shows and what appears in the simulation output header. The diff indicates MaxNumUsersPaired=12, MaxNumLayers=24, and SemiOrthogonalityFactor=0.5, yet the simulation results display в‰¤2 paired users, в‰¤16 layers, and Semiortho=0.7. This suggests either the HEAD commit differs from what's currently running, or there's a mismatch between the working directory and what was actually executed.

For the summary the user wants, I should trace the progression from April 13 through the current state: starting with severe BLER issues in the 35-60% range on CDL-B, then moving through OLLA state analysis, implementing State 3 without OLLA reset on CSI reports, adding per-frame BLER logging, restricting rank to в‰¤2, reducing MaxNumUsersPaired from 4 to 2, and finally switching to CDL-D channel model. I notice there are additional OLLA-related changes in nrComponentCarrierContext.m that might be relevant to understanding the current behavior.

Looking at the git diff, I see the MCS offset reset logic with a comment explaining that each CSI period is treated as an independent OLLA window with fresh CQI measurements. This appears to be the original reset behavior, not the State 3 no-reset approach we discussed earlier. Yet the most recent results show OLLA converging well in the last few frames, which suggests the current configuration is working effectively despite this reset behavior. I'm parsing through the git diff more carefully now. The reset line is actually still present in the HEAD versionвЂ”it's just the comment that changed. The commit message says it fixed the OLLA reset, but looking at the actual code, the `obj.MCSOffset(1) = schedulerConfig.LinkAdaptationConfigUL.InitialOffset;` line remains in both versions, which means the reset is still happening on each CSI report.

The key insight is that the working tree has a different state than what's committedвЂ”we've removed that reset line locally in State 3, but git HEAD still contains it. So the CDL-D results showing OLLA convergence must be from running with our local modification that removes the reset, not from the committed code.

Now I'm piecing together the actual timeline of changes we've made to understand what produced these results.

We started with a 35-60% BLER problem back in April, which led us to discover that the OLLA was resetting its MCS offset every CSI periodвЂ”preventing it from learning across multiple measurements. The core issue was that MU-MIMO CQI measurements are taken in single-user mode but applied to multi-user scenarios, so they overestimate the actual SINR and need time to accumulate corrections. We fixed this by removing the reset in the component carrier context, then added detailed per-frame logging to track BLER and MCS changes. Finally, we restricted the rank to 2 or less since higher ranks weren't helping with the channel conditions we're seeing.

The real breakthrough came when we switched from CDL-B to CDL-DвЂ”the line-of-sight dominant channel model aligns much better with how the codebook-based MU-MIMO precoding works, and that's when OLLA finally converged with BLER dropping to around 8% over the last few frames.
